cards.question: Which pattern converts one library's interface to another without changing consumer code?

options.B: Adapter

cards.answer_text: Adapter
cards.explanation: Adapter converts interface A to interface B so consumers depend on target interface. Facade simplifies complex subsystem. Wrapper composes component with app defaults. Decorator extends behavior.

cards.question: Your team uses three different chart libraries. You need a unified API for bar/line/pie charts. Which pattern?

options.A: Adapter per library — each implements a common IChart interface
options.B: Single Facade over all three libraries
options.D: No abstraction — let each team choose their own API

cards.answer_text: Adapter per library — each implements a common IChart interface
cards.explanation: Adapter converts each library's API to common IChart interface. Facade would hide too much. Wrapper per chart type duplicates effort. No abstraction makes migration impossible.

cards.question: Which is a sign of a 'thin wrapper' anti-pattern?

options.A: Wrapper adds default props and constrains options
options.B: Wrapper passes every prop through without transformation
options.C: Wrapper provides an escape hatch to native library instance
options.D: Wrapper is used in exactly one component

cards.answer_text: Wrapper passes every prop through without transformation
cards.explanation: Thin wrapper passes all props unchanged — adds indirection without value. Defaults, constraints, and escape hatches are useful additions. Usage count does not determine thickness.

cards.question: When should you skip wrapping a library?

options.B: Library is a de-facto standard like React itself
options.D: Team expects to evaluate alternatives next quarter

cards.answer_text: Library is a de-facto standard like React itself
cards.explanation: De-facto standards with near-zero migration probability (React, TypeScript) do not need wrapping. Libraries with broad usage, complex lifecycle, or potential replacement candidates should be wrapped.

cards.question: A Facade pattern exposes getNativeInstance() for advanced use. How to manage this?

options.B: Document that escape hatch voids abstraction guarantees and track usage in code review
options.C: Make getNativeInstance() return a proxied version that logs usage
options.D: Only expose getNativeInstance() in development builds

cards.answer_text: Document that escape hatch voids abstraction guarantees and track usage in code review
cards.explanation: Documenting and tracking escape hatch usage is pragmatic. Removing it hurts power users. Proxying/logging adds complexity. Dev-only breaks production edge cases.

cards.question: Which scenario makes abstraction pay off most?

options.A: Library is universally used by all teams for diverse needs
options.B: Library is used in one file by one developer
options.C: Library has high migration likelihood and is used across modules
options.D: Library is extremely simple (< 5 API methods)

cards.answer_text: Library has high migration likelihood and is used across modules
cards.explanation: Abstraction pays off when library has realistic replacement candidates AND broad usage. Single-file usage: refactor when migration happens. Simple libs: direct dependency fine.

cards.question: A wrapper exposes library-specific prop names (e.g., 'freezeColumns' for AG Grid). What anti-pattern is this?

options.B: Leaky abstraction

cards.answer_text: Leaky abstraction
cards.explanation: Leaky abstraction exposes library-specific concepts to consumers. FreezeColumns is AG Grid concept. Clean abstraction would expose 'columns: { frozen: boolean }' or similar app-level concept.

cards.question: Why is adding abstraction during migration problematic?

options.A: Migration is when you most need abstraction
options.B: You discover needed API surface only mid-migration, leading to adapter that mimics old API
options.C: Abstraction during migration is always correct
options.D: Libraries do not support abstraction during migration

cards.answer_text: You discover needed API surface only mid-migration, leading to adapter that mimics old API
cards.explanation: Adding abstraction during migration is reactive — you build interface based on old library's quirks instead of clean domain interface. Create abstraction when library usage stabilizes.

cards.question: What distinguishes Facade from Adapter?

options.A: Facade converts interface, Adapter simplifies subsystem
options.B: Facade simplifies complex subsystem, Adapter converts one interface to another
options.D: Facade wraps component, Adapter wraps API

cards.answer_text: Facade simplifies complex subsystem, Adapter converts one interface to another
cards.explanation: Facade provides simplified view of complex subsystem (reducing surface area). Adapter converts one interface to another (preserving intent, changing shape). Both are structural patterns with different intents.

cards.question: Your team's single chart wrapper handles Recharts, visx, and D3. It has 2000 LOC and 15 configuration props. What anti-pattern?

options.A: Thin wrapper — still not abstracting enough
options.B: God abstraction — handles too many libraries in one component
options.D: This is correct — single wrapper for all charts

cards.answer_text: God abstraction — handles too many libraries in one component
cards.explanation: God abstraction tries to cover every library's capabilities in one component. Better: adapter per library (each implements IChart interface). Simpler, testable, independently migratable.

cards.question: What is the primary benefit of abstracting column definitions into AppColumnDef?

options.B: Switching grid libraries changes only the mapper functions
options.C: TypeScript can infer row type from column definitions

cards.answer_text: Switching grid libraries changes only the mapper functions
cards.explanation: AppColumnDef provides library-agnostic column schema. Mapper functions convert to library-specific format. Migration changes mappers, not every column usage.

cards.question: A grid library owns its internal sort state. Why externalize it?

options.A: Library internal state is always buggy
options.B: External state enables URL sync, undo, and multi-grid coordination
options.D: Library state cannot be serialized to JSON

cards.answer_text: External state enables URL sync, undo, and multi-grid coordination
cards.explanation: External state enables URL deep linking, undo/redo, sharing sort/filter between grids, and cross-tab synchronization. Library internal state is isolated.

cards.question: Which approach handles library-specific grid features (e.g., AG Grid pivot) in an abstraction?

options.B: Abstract only the intersection of features across supported libraries
options.C: Expose every library feature as optional props
options.D: Only support features present in all libraries

cards.answer_text: Abstract only the intersection of features across supported libraries
cards.explanation: Abstract the intersection. For library-specific features, provide optional escape hatch (e.g., `extra` prop typed as `Record<string, unknown>`). This prevents feature lock-in while allowing full library use.

cards.question: TanStack Table is headless — it provides hooks, not UI. What additional library does a headless grid need?

options.B: TanStack Virtual (or similar virtualizer)

cards.answer_text: TanStack Virtual (or similar virtualizer)
cards.explanation: Headless table provides logic (sort, filter, pagination) but no rendering. Virtualizer handles windowed rendering so only visible rows are in DOM. Required for performance with large datasets.

cards.question: AG Grid community license lacks pivot and Excel export. How to design wrapper for future enterprise upgrade?

options.A: Use AG Grid enterprise from the start
options.B: Design IEnterpriseFeature interface, implement with community stubs, swap for enterprise implementation later
options.C: Avoid AG Grid — only use TanStack Table
options.D: Fork AG Grid community and add features

cards.answer_text: Design IEnterpriseFeature interface, implement with community stubs, swap for enterprise implementation later
cards.explanation: IEnterpriseFeature adapter pattern. Community stub throws 'not implemented' or returns unsupported. Enterprise implementation delegates to AG Grid enterprise. Conditional import via dynamic import or build flag.

cards.question: When is controlled grid state problematic for performance?

options.A: Always — controlled state is always slower
options.B: When wrapper re-renders all rows on every sort change instead of delegating to grid's virtual DOM
options.D: When state is serialized to URL

cards.answer_text: When wrapper re-renders all rows on every sort change instead of delegating to grid's virtual DOM
cards.explanation: Controlled state is fine if wrapper passes state to grid library's optimized internal mechanisms. Problematic if wrapper triggers full React re-render of visible rows on every state mutation. Use React.memo and library-specific batch updates.

cards.question: Wrapper exposes 'pivot' prop even though only AG Grid enterprise supports it. What is this?

options.B: Leaky abstraction — app-level concerns coupled to library feature
options.C: Protection — consumers should know all options
options.D: Feature parity — all grids should have pivot

cards.answer_text: Leaky abstraction — app-level concerns coupled to library feature
cards.explanation: Pivot is a library-specific concept, not an app-level grid concern. Exposing it in wrapper API couples abstraction to AG Grid enterprise. Better: abstract feature behind generic concept like 'aggregate' or 'cross-tab'.

cards.question: How to handle inline editing validation across different grid libraries?

options.A: Each library handles its own validation — no abstraction needed
options.B: Define GridEditConfig with validate function — wrapper maps to library validation API
options.C: Validation must be done outside grid, after save
options.D: Only allow editing in AG Grid (turnkey solution)

cards.answer_text: Define GridEditConfig with validate function — wrapper maps to library validation API
cards.explanation: GridEditConfig provides uniform validation model. Mapper converts to library-specific validation mechanism (AG Grid cellClassRules, TanStack custom editor with validation). Consumer writes validation once.

cards.question: How does virtual scrolling in AG Grid differ from TanStack Table?

options.A: AG Grid virtualizes internally; TanStack Table requires external virtualizer
options.B: TanStack Table virtualizes internally; AG Grid requires external virtualizer
options.D: Neither virtualizes — you must use react-window

cards.answer_text: AG Grid virtualizes internally; TanStack Table requires external virtualizer
cards.explanation: AG Grid handles virtual scrolling internally — you get it for free. TanStack Table is headless — you pair it with TanStack Virtual or react-window. Wrapper should hide this difference.

cards.question: Your grid wrapper exposes 'aggregation' concept. AG Grid has built-in aggregation. TanStack Table requires manual reduce. How to implement?

options.A: Implement aggregation manually for all libraries
options.B: Use AG Grid aggregation when available, manual reduce for TanStack — both behind same aggregation interface
options.C: Skip aggregation in wrapper — consumers implement separately per library
options.D: Only support aggregation with AG Grid

cards.answer_text: Use AG Grid aggregation when available, manual reduce for TanStack — both behind same aggregation interface
cards.explanation: Abstract concept ('aggregation') maps to library-specific implementation. AG Grid uses built-in aggregate functions. TanStack uses reduce over row model. Both implement the same interface. Consumer gets aggregation without knowing which strategy runs.

cards.question: Which form paradigm gives the most control over field layout and styling?

options.B: Headless (React Hook Form, Formik)
options.D: Both A and B give equal control

cards.answer_text: Headless (React Hook Form, Formik)
cards.explanation: Headless form libraries manage state only. Consumer renders every field, controls layout, styling, and behavior. Schema-driven libraries auto-render from JSON schema, limiting layout control.

cards.question: When should you choose Formio over React Hook Form?

options.B: When forms are schema-driven, stored in DB, or configured by non-developers
options.C: When bundle size must be minimal
options.D: When you need custom field validation

cards.answer_text: When forms are schema-driven, stored in DB, or configured by non-developers
cards.explanation: Formio excels at dynamic forms rendered from persisted schemas — admin panels, surveys, user-configurable forms. RHF excels at lightweight, custom-layout forms.

cards.question: How should validation logic be structured to remain form-library-agnostic?

options.A: Use built-in validation of chosen form library
options.B: Define validation as pure functions with typed input/output, adapt to form library via resolver
options.C: Duplicate validation in both form library and backend

cards.answer_text: Define validation as pure functions with typed input/output, adapt to form library via resolver
cards.explanation: Pure validation functions (Zod schema, custom validator) have no form library dependency. Adapter converts result to library-specific format (RHF resolver, Formio custom validation). Swap adapter when library changes.

cards.question: A wizard form uses RHF internally. How does state persist across wizard steps?

options.A: Each step has independent state — merge on final submit
options.B: Single useForm instance shared across all steps — each step renders subset of fields
options.C: Store state in URL search params
options.D: Wizard state is managed by a separate library (e.g., Zustand)

cards.answer_text: Single useForm instance shared across all steps — each step renders subset of fields
cards.explanation: Single useForm instance holds all fields. Each step calls register() for its subset of fields. trigger() validates current step. Final submit calls handleSubmit() with all fields.

cards.question: What is the primary cost of schema-driven forms?

options.B: Limited layout control and larger bundle size

cards.answer_text: Limited layout control and larger bundle size
cards.explanation: Schema-driven forms trade layout flexibility for automation. Formio bundle is ~200KB. Custom layouts require renderer customization or component overrides. Accessibility depends on renderer quality.

cards.question: Validation needs cross-field rules (e.g., endDate > startDate). Which approach works for both RHF and Formio?

options.B: Define cross-field rule in Zod/yup schema — both RHF resolver and Formio custom validation can call it

cards.answer_text: Define cross-field rule in Zod/yup schema — both RHF resolver and Formio custom validation can call it
cards.explanation: Pure validation function (Zod refine, yup.test) works in both. RHF calls via resolver. Formio calls via onSubmit custom validation handler. Write once, adapt twice.

cards.question: Form interface FormContext<T> abstracts form state. What is its main benefit?

options.B: Lets consumers switch form library without changing form interaction code

cards.answer_text: Lets consumers switch form library without changing form interaction code
cards.explanation: FormContext<T> standardizes form operations (setValue, submit, errors, isValid). Components depend on context interface, not library-specific hooks. Change library: provide new FormContext implementation.

cards.question: A multi-step form needs server validation per step. How to design?

options.A: Submit all steps at once — server validates everything
options.B: Two-tier validation: client-side Zod per step + server validation on step POST, merge errors back
options.C: Skip client-side validation — server validates each step
options.D: Validate only on final submit — no per-step validation

cards.answer_text: Two-tier validation: client-side Zod per step + server validation on step POST, merge errors back
cards.explanation: Client-side validation provides instant feedback. Server validation per step catches business rule violations. Merged errors let user fix per-step issues before proceeding. Both validations must pass for step to advance.

cards.question: Which pattern lets you mix headless and schema-driven forms in same app?

options.A: Wrap both behind same FormContext interface
options.B: Use only one form library per app
options.C: Use iframes to isolate form paradigms

cards.answer_text: Wrap both behind same FormContext interface
cards.explanation: FormContext abstraction hides implementation. RHF and Formio both implement FormContext. Consumer components use FormContext regardless of which library runs which form.

cards.question: A designer wants fine control over form field placement across multiple columns. Which form paradigm fits best?

options.A: Schema-driven — columns defined in JSON layout
options.B: Headless — consumer renders fields in CSS Grid with full layout control
options.C: Neither — forms cannot support multi-column layouts

cards.answer_text: Headless — consumer renders fields in CSS Grid with full layout control
cards.explanation: Headless forms give consumer full layout control (CSS Grid, Flexbox, custom breakpoints). Schema-driven forms have opinionated layout engines that make pixel-perfect multi-column layouts difficult without customization.

cards.question: Which DnD library is currently recommended for new projects?

options.B: dnd-kit

cards.answer_text: dnd-kit
cards.explanation: dnd-kit is actively maintained, modular, accessible, and supports complex DnD (tree, kanban). react-beautiful-dnd is deprecated. jQuery UI is legacy. HTML Drag and Drop API has inconsistent browser support and poor accessibility.

cards.question: What is the primary purpose of dnd-kit's DragOverlay?

options.B: Renders a portal-based preview of dragged item on top of all content
options.C: Provides keyboard navigation for drag operations

cards.answer_text: Renders a portal-based preview of dragged item on top of all content
cards.explanation: DragOverlay renders a copy of the dragged item via portal, tracking cursor position. This lets the original item remain in place (or show placeholder) while dragged copy floats above everything.

cards.question: How does dnd-kit implement keyboard-accessible DnD?

options.A: KeyboardSensor — maps arrow keys to drag movement
options.B: Keyboard DnD is not supported
options.C: Uses native HTML5 drag and drop keyboard support
options.D: Separate ARIA buttons for move up/down

cards.answer_text: KeyboardSensor — maps arrow keys to drag movement
cards.explanation: dnd-kit's KeyboardSensor lets users activate drag with Space/Enter, move with arrow keys, cancel with Escape. UseSensors composes PointerSensor + KeyboardSensor.

cards.question: In a kanban board with multiple SortableContexts, how does dnd-kit determine which container receives a dropped item?

options.A: Uses collision detection algorithms (closestCenter, closestCorners, pointerWithin)
options.B: Each container is a separate DndContext

cards.answer_text: Uses collision detection algorithms (closestCenter, closestCorners, pointerWithin)
cards.explanation: Collision detection (closestCenter, closestCorners, pointerWithin) identifies target container based on pointer position relative to droppable areas. Multiple SortableContexts share one DndContext for cross-container moves.

cards.question: How to announce DnD state changes to screen readers in dnd-kit?

options.A: Cannot — dnd-kit does not support screen readers
options.B: Via DndContext.announcements prop — maps dragStart/dragOver/dragEnd/dragCancel to messages
options.C: Screen readers automatically detect drag operations

cards.answer_text: Via DndContext.announcements prop — maps dragStart/dragOver/dragEnd/dragCancel to messages
cards.explanation: DndContext.announcements is a map of lifecycle events to screen reader messages. Customize per app context (e.g., 'Moved column 3 to position 5').

cards.question: DnD wrapper exposes onDragEnd handler with dnd-kit's DragEndEvent type. What abstraction concern?

options.B: Leaky abstraction — consumer depends on library types
options.C: DragEndEvent is standard — no abstraction issue
options.D: Should use any type to avoid coupling

cards.answer_text: Leaky abstraction — consumer depends on library types
cards.explanation: Exposing library-specific event types couples consumer to dnd-kit. Better: normalize to app-level shape like `{ activeId, overId, sourceIndex, destIndex }`. Library change then only affects wrapper internals.

cards.question: How to handle drag within a virtualized list (TanStack Virtual)?

options.B: Align dnd-kit's MeasuringStrategy with virtualizer's measurement. Use Always strategy for dynamic heights
options.D: Use react-beautiful-dnd instead — it handles virtualization natively

cards.answer_text: Align dnd-kit's MeasuringStrategy with virtualizer's measurement. Use Always strategy for dynamic heights
cards.explanation: dnd-kit measures item layout. Virtualizer also measures. Conflicting measurements cause jumpy drag. Use `MeasuringStrategy.Always` in dnd-kit and ensure both use same item size calculations.

cards.question: What is the relationship between DndContext and SortableContext in dnd-kit?

options.B: DndContext provides drag infrastructure; SortableContext configures sortable behavior for a group of items
options.D: SortableContext provides collision detection; DndContext provides sensors

cards.answer_text: DndContext provides drag infrastructure; SortableContext configures sortable behavior for a group of items
cards.explanation: One DndContext wraps entire drag region. Multiple SortableContexts inside define sortable groups (lists/columns). DndContext handles sensors, collision detection, lifecycle. SortableContext handles item ordering strategy.

cards.question: When would you choose up/down buttons over drag-and-drop for reordering?

options.B: When accessibility compliance requires keyboard-only operation
options.C: For simple lists where DnD's overhead (drag overlay, sensor config, collision detection) is not justified
options.D: Never — drag-and-drop is always better UX

cards.answer_text: For simple lists where DnD's overhead (drag overlay, sensor config, collision detection) is not justified
cards.explanation: For simple reordering (short lists, admin CRUD), up/down buttons are simpler to implement and fully accessible. DnD's overhead is justified for: visual canvas, kanban, resizable panels, or user expectation of drag.

cards.question: A wrapper includes DndContext with keyboard sensor but no announcements. What issue?

options.A: No issue — keyboard sensor alone provides accessibility
options.B: Keyboard sensor without announcements means screen reader users get no feedback on drag state changes
options.C: Announcements are only for pointer drag
options.D: Keyboard sensor requires announcements to function

cards.answer_text: Keyboard sensor without announcements means screen reader users get no feedback on drag state changes
cards.explanation: Keyboard sensor enables keyboard operation. Announcements provide feedback ('dragging item X', 'dropped at position Y'). Without announcements, screen reader users do not know what happened during drag.

cards.question: Which editor engine is best suited for complex collaborative document editing?

options.B: TipTap (ProseMirror) — mature Y.js integration and complex node support

cards.answer_text: TipTap (ProseMirror) — mature Y.js integration and complex node support
cards.explanation: TipTap's ProseMirror foundation has most mature Y.js collaboration, custom node support (tables, embeds, code blocks), and battle-tested in production editors (Notion-style apps). Lexical collab is newer.

cards.question: Why should editor content be stored as JSON, not HTML?

options.B: HTML loses semantic structure (e.g., cannot distinguish bold-in-heading from bold-in-paragraph)
options.D: HTML cannot represent mentions or custom nodes

cards.answer_text: HTML loses semantic structure (e.g., cannot distinguish bold-in-heading from bold-in-paragraph)
cards.explanation: HTML is a rendering format — it loses structural semantics. JSON preserves node types, marks, attributes. HTML storage also risks XSS. Store JSON, render to HTML for display.

cards.question: In controlled editor mode, who owns the document state?

options.A: Editor — app reads value via ref on demand
options.B: App — editor calls onChange on every mutation, app feeds value prop back
options.C: Both — state is synced via two-way binding

cards.answer_text: App — editor calls onChange on every mutation, app feeds value prop back
cards.explanation: Controlled mode: app stores document state (e.g., useState). Editor is passive — receives value prop, fires onChange. App filters, debounces, or saves onChange as needed.

cards.question: How should custom mentions (e.g., @user) be abstracted across editors?

options.A: Implement per-editor — too different to abstract
options.B: MentionProvider interface — wrapper wires to editor-specific mechanism
options.D: Use HTML data attributes — universal across editors

cards.answer_text: MentionProvider interface — wrapper wires to editor-specific mechanism
cards.explanation: MentionProvider defines search/format contract. TipTap adapter uses Suggestion plugin. Lexical adapter uses MentionNode + custom logic. Consumer implements MentionProvider once.

cards.question: What does TipTap's extension model add over Lexical extensions?

options.A: TipTap extensions can modify ProseMirror schema, input rules, paste rules, and keymaps — full ProseMirror power
options.B: TipTap extensions are simpler than Lexical
options.C: TipTap does not have extensions — only nodes

cards.answer_text: TipTap extensions can modify ProseMirror schema, input rules, paste rules, and keymaps — full ProseMirror power
cards.explanation: TipTap extensions are ProseMirror plugins with schema, input rules, paste rules, keymaps, commands, shortcuts, and lifecycle hooks. Lexical nodes/plugins are simpler but less extensible for complex document behavior.

cards.question: A normalized EditorDocument model is lossy — some editor-specific features do not map. When is this acceptable?

options.B: When app stores documents in DB and may migrate editors later
options.D: Only for preview rendering, not for storage

cards.answer_text: When app stores documents in DB and may migrate editors later
cards.explanation: Lossy normalization is acceptable for storage portability. Features unique to current editor (custom node types, plugin data) can be stored in an 'extra' field on the normalized schema. Trade precision for migration flexibility.

cards.question: Which editor supports collaborative editing via Y.js?

options.B: Both TipTap and Lexical (Lexical >=0.12)
options.D: Neither — Y.js is a separate framework

cards.answer_text: Both TipTap and Lexical (Lexical >=0.12)
cards.explanation: TipTap uses @tiptap/y for Y.js integration. Lexical v0.12+ has @lexical/yjs for Y.js. Collaboration config (provider, room, awareness) can be abstracted across both.

cards.question: Where should custom extension logic (e.g., @mention querying users) live?

options.B: Outside wrapper in app code — extension definition is app concern, wrapper provides registration interface
options.C: In the backend — frontend should only display results

cards.answer_text: Outside wrapper in app code — extension definition is app concern, wrapper provides registration interface
cards.explanation: Extension definition (what it does) is app logic. Wrapper provides registerExtension(type, adapter) interface. Adapter (how editor registers it) is wrapper concern. Clear separation.

cards.question: Should image upload logic live inside editor wrapper?

options.B: No — UploadHandler interface injected into wrapper; upload implementation is app concern
options.C: Images should not be uploaded from editor
options.D: Upload URL is enough — no handler needed

cards.answer_text: No — UploadHandler interface injected into wrapper; upload implementation is app concern
cards.explanation: UploadHandler interface isolates upload destination, auth, and progress tracking from editor logic. App implements handler (S3, Cloudinary, local server). Wrapper calls handler during image insertion.

cards.question: When should you skip rich text and use a plain textarea or Markdown editor?

options.A: When app needs mentions or slash commands
options.B: When app users only need simple input (name, description) with no formatting requirements
options.D: Both B and C — rich text complexity unjustified for simple text input or mobile editing

cards.answer_text: Both B and C — rich text complexity unjustified for simple text input or mobile editing
cards.explanation: Rich text editors are complex and hard to use on mobile. If app only needs simple text input or targets mobile users, Markdown or plain textarea is better. Rich text justified for: document creation, formatted content, collaborative writing.

cards.question: Which chart library category gives full control over SVG rendering?

options.B: Composable (visx)

cards.answer_text: Imperative (D3)
cards.explanation: D3 gives full imperative control over every SVG element. Recharts abstracts SVG. visx sits between — composable but still built on D3 primitives.

cards.question: Where should data transformation (filtering, aggregation, sorting) live?

options.B: Separate pipeline function — chart wrapper accepts ready-to-render data

cards.answer_text: Separate pipeline function — chart wrapper accepts ready-to-render data
cards.explanation: Separate data pipeline keeps transformation testable and reusable across charts. Wrapper remains a pure renderer. Pipeline can be shared between chart and non-chart views (table, export).

cards.question: What is the purpose of ChartTheme abstraction?

options.B: Provides consistent colors, fonts, and grid styles across chart libraries

cards.answer_text: Provides consistent colors, fonts, and grid styles across chart libraries
cards.explanation: ChartTheme defines uniform visual properties mapped to library-specific theme structures. Swap library, map same theme to new structure — charts look identical.

cards.question: When would you use raw D3 instead of Recharts?

options.B: For standard bar/line/pie charts
options.C: For novel visualizations (force-directed graph, chord diagram, custom cartography)

cards.answer_text: For novel visualizations (force-directed graph, chord diagram, custom cartography)
cards.explanation: D3 for novel visualizations that declarative libraries cannot express. Recharts/visx for standard business charts. D3 for custom; declarative for standard.

cards.question: How to handle responsive chart resizing across libraries?

options.A: Use each library's built-in responsive component (Recharts ResponsiveContainer, visx ParentSize)
options.B: Single ResizeObserver wrapper that provides width/height to any chart library

cards.answer_text: Single ResizeObserver wrapper that provides width/height to any chart library
cards.explanation: Single ResizeObserver wrapper normalizes responsive behavior. All chart libraries receive same width/height. Avoids library-specific responsive quirks and provides uniform API.

cards.question: Migrating from Recharts to visx. What changes are needed outside chart wrapper?

options.B: Only wrapper internals change — ChartConfig API stays

cards.answer_text: Only wrapper internals change — ChartConfig API stays
cards.explanation: With ChartConfig abstraction, library swap changes wrapper internals (config-to-JSX mapping). ChartConfig interface, data pipeline, and theme definitions remain unchanged.

cards.question: ChartConfig.extra allows per-library features. What concern?

options.A: Too flexible — every chart becomes unique, losing abstraction benefit
options.B: Perfect — escape hatch for library-specific features
options.C: Extra props should go in separate file

cards.answer_text: Too flexible — every chart becomes unique, losing abstraction benefit
cards.explanation: If every chart uses extra for different features, wrapper adds no value. Better: promote commonly-used extra features to first-class ChartConfig props. Extra is escape hatch, not primary API.

cards.question: Which chart export approach is abstraction-friendly?

options.A: Use library-specific export methods directly in components
options.B: ChartExport interface: toImage(), toSVG() implemented per library

cards.answer_text: ChartExport interface: toImage(), toSVG() implemented per library
cards.explanation: ChartExport interface wraps library-specific export. Recharts uses html2canvas. visx uses SVG serialization. D3 uses XMLSerializer. Consumer calls export.toImage() regardless of library.

cards.question: Dashboard uses 5 chart types, all standard (bar, line, pie, area, scatter). Which library choice is most practical?

options.A: Raw D3 for all — full control
options.B: Recharts or visx — standard chart types well-covered, less code than D3

cards.answer_text: Recharts or visx — standard chart types well-covered, less code than D3
cards.explanation: Standard chart types are well-covered by declarative/composable libraries. Recharts or visx produce correct charts with 80% less code than D3. Only switch to D3 for non-standard visualizations.

cards.question: Animation config is part of ChartConfig. How to keep it library-agnostic?

options.A: Each library has unique animation API — cannot abstract
options.B: Abstract as { duration: number, easing: string, type: 'fade' | 'scale' | 'slide' } — wrapper maps to library animation
options.D: Animation is a render-time concern — not part of config

cards.answer_text: Abstract as { duration: number, easing: string, type: 'fade' | 'scale' | 'slide' } — wrapper maps to library animation
cards.explanation: Common animation properties (duration, easing, type) abstract across libraries. Mapper converts to Recharts animationDuration/easing, visx animationTiming, D3 transition duration/ease. Library-specific animation details stay in wrapper.

cards.question: Which map library renders markers as WebGL (not DOM elements)?

options.B: MapLibre GL JS

cards.answer_text: MapLibre GL JS
cards.explanation: MapLibre uses WebGL for all rendering — markers, lines, polygons are drawn on GPU. Leaflet creates DOM elements per marker (performance limit with thousands of markers).

cards.question: What is the primary performance limitation of Leaflet with 10k+ markers?

options.B: 10k+ DOM elements cause scroll jank and memory pressure
options.C: Leaflet cannot load more than 5k markers

cards.answer_text: 10k+ DOM elements cause scroll jank and memory pressure
cards.explanation: Leaflet creates DOM elements (div or img) for each marker. 10k DOM nodes causes slow rendering, scrolling jank, and high memory usage. MapLibre's WebGL rendering avoids DOM overhead.

cards.question: In map lifecycle React hook, when should map.remove() be called?

options.B: In useEffect cleanup — before component unmount
options.C: map.remove() is not needed — garbage collection handles it

cards.answer_text: In useEffect cleanup — before component unmount
cards.explanation: Map instances hold WebGL contexts, tile cache, and event listeners. Without cleanup in useEffect return, multiple map instances accumulate on re-renders, causing memory leaks.

cards.question: MapInstance.getNative() exposes underlying library instance. When is this acceptable?

options.B: For library-specific features not covered by MapInstance interface, with clear documentation that it is migration liability
options.C: Always — full library access is expected

cards.answer_text: For library-specific features not covered by MapInstance interface, with clear documentation that it is migration liability
cards.explanation: getNative() is escape hatch for features not in MapInstance interface (e.g., MapLibre 3D terrain, Leaflet heatmap plugin config). Document usage and limit to unavoidable cases.

cards.question: You need 100 markers with popups on a map page. Which library choice is appropriate?

options.B: Leaflet — lightweight, DOM handles 100 markers fine
options.D: Both A and B work; Leaflet is simpler for this scale

cards.answer_text: Both A and B work; Leaflet is simpler for this scale
cards.explanation: 100 markers is well within Leaflet's capability. Leaflet is lighter bundle and simpler API. MapLibre's WebGL is unnecessary. Abstraction lets you start with Leaflet, upgrade to MapLibre if data grows.

cards.question: How does MapLibre handle heatmap rendering differently from Leaflet?

options.B: MapLibre has native heatmap layer type (WebGL); Leaflet requires a third-party plugin
options.D: Leaflet has built-in heatmap; MapLibre requires plugin

cards.answer_text: MapLibre has native heatmap layer type (WebGL); Leaflet requires a third-party plugin
cards.explanation: MapLibre supports heatmap as built-in layer type via WebGL fragment shaders. Leaflet heatmap requires external plugin (Leaflet.heat). Abstraction should include plugin registration in map factory.

cards.question: 50k GPS points must display on map with clustering. Best approach?

options.B: MapLibre with built-in GeoJSON clustering
options.D: Load all points as individual markers

cards.answer_text: MapLibre with built-in GeoJSON clustering
cards.explanation: MapLibre's WebGL + built-in clustering handles 50k points efficiently. Leaflet MarkerCluster would create many DOM cluster elements, causing performance issues. MapLibre clustering is native and GPU-accelerated.

cards.question: What is viewport filtering used for?

options.A: Filtering data points that are outside current map view, reducing render load
options.B: Applying CSS filters to map tiles

cards.answer_text: Filtering data points that are outside current map view, reducing render load
cards.explanation: Viewport filtering only renders data within current map bounds. Essential for 100k+ datasets where even WebGL struggles. Paired with viewport-based API queries for server-side filtering.

cards.question: How should tile provider switching (OpenStreetMap → Mapbox) be handled?

options.A: Change in MapConfig.style — MapLibre uses GL JSON, Leaflet uses tile URL template
options.B: Each tile provider requires separate map implementation
options.C: Cannot switch tile providers after initialization
options.D: Tile provider is not a map concern

cards.answer_text: Change in MapConfig.style — MapLibre uses GL JSON, Leaflet uses tile URL template
cards.explanation: MapConfig.style abstracts tile source. MapLibre accepts GL style URL/JSON. Leaflet accepts tile URL template. Provider change = config change. Wrapper passes config to respective library's style mechanism.

cards.question: A map wrapper uses only the intersection of MapLibre and Leaflet features. What limitation?

options.A: Cannot use MapLibre 3D terrain, heatmap, or custom GL layers through wrapper
options.B: Maps will not render
options.D: No limitation — intersection covers all use cases

cards.answer_text: Cannot use MapLibre 3D terrain, heatmap, or custom GL layers through wrapper
cards.explanation: Lowest-common-denominator abstraction misses MapLibre's advanced features (3D terrain, custom GL layers, satellite imagery). Wrapper must provide getNative() or optional advanced config for library-specific features.

cards.question: How many DOM nodes does a virtual scroll list typically render regardless of total items?

options.A: All items — virtual scrolling does not reduce DOM nodes
options.B: Only visible items + overscan buffer (typically 10-30 nodes)

cards.answer_text: Only visible items + overscan buffer (typically 10-30 nodes)
cards.explanation: Virtual scroll renders only visible viewport items plus overscan (items just above/below). With 100k items, typically 10-30 DOM nodes. Significantly reduces render time and memory.

cards.question: What is the overscan property in TanStack Virtual?

options.A: Number of items to skip before rendering starts
options.B: Extra items rendered above and below viewport to reduce blank areas during fast scroll
options.C: Maximum number of items to render
options.D: Percentage of container height to reserve

cards.answer_text: Extra items rendered above and below viewport to reduce blank areas during fast scroll
cards.explanation: Overscan renders extra items beyond viewport so fast scrolling does not show blank space while new items load. Higher overscan = smoother scroll but more DOM nodes.

cards.question: How does TanStack Virtual handle variable item heights?

options.A: All items must be same height
options.B: estimateSize provides initial estimate; measureElement callback measures actual height after render
options.D: Developer must pre-calculate all heights before rendering

cards.answer_text: estimateSize provides initial estimate; measureElement callback measures actual height after render
cards.explanation: estimateSize gives initial guess. measureElement reads actual DOM height after render. Cached per index. Re-measure on content change.

cards.question: When combining infinite scroll with virtual scrolling, where does the endReached callback trigger?

options.A: When user scrolls to last item
options.B: When virtual scroll's last item enters viewport minus threshold distance

cards.answer_text: When virtual scroll's last item enters viewport minus threshold distance
cards.explanation: endReached fires when scroll position is within threshold px of total content bottom. Virtual scroll's total size includes loaded items; endReached detects when user approaches loaded boundary.

cards.question: Chat prepends messages when scrolling up. How to prevent scroll position jump?

options.A: Cannot — virtual scroll cannot handle prepended items
options.B: After prepend, increase scrollTop by total height of new items

cards.answer_text: After prepend, increase scrollTop by total height of new items
cards.explanation: Prepending shifts all items down. Adjust scrollTop by sum of new item heights (from estimate or measure). Virtualizer re-indexes. User stays at same logical position.

cards.question: What must be true for virtual scrolling to work?

options.A: Container must have a fixed or computed height (not auto)
options.B: Container must be the document body

cards.answer_text: Container must have a fixed or computed height (not auto)
cards.explanation: Virtual scrolling needs scrollable container with defined height. Height: auto means container grows with content — cannot virtualize. Set explicit height or use flex-basis + overflow.

cards.question: VirtualList wrapper hides TanStack Virtual API. How to allow scrollToIndex?

options.A: Expose scrollToIndex as prop that changes trigger scroll
options.B: Provide onVirtualizerReady callback that gives consumer access to virtualizer
options.D: Consumer must use native scroll method on container ref

cards.answer_text: Provide onVirtualizerReady callback that gives consumer access to virtualizer
cards.explanation: onVirtualizerReady callback provides controlled access to virtualizer for advanced operations (scrollToIndex, getTotalSize). Used in conjunction with imperative handle (useImperativeHandle).

cards.question: How to restore scroll position when navigating back to a list?

options.B: Save scrollTop to sessionStorage on unmount, restore on mount
options.C: Use browser History API scroll restoration

cards.answer_text: Save scrollTop to sessionStorage on unmount, restore on mount
cards.explanation: Save scrollRef.current.scrollTop to sessionStorage/navigation state before unmount. On mount, read saved value and set scrollRef.current.scrollTop = saved. browser History API scrollRestoration = 'manual' to prevent conflict.

cards.question: When should you skip virtual scrolling?

options.B: When list has <100 items and rendering all is performant

cards.answer_text: When list has <100 items and rendering all is performant
cards.explanation: Virtual scroll overhead (measurement, re-renders, scroll synchronization) is unnecessary for small lists. For <100 items, rendering all is simpler and performant.

cards.question: Virtual scroll with animated item enter/exit (Framer Motion) is tricky because?

options.A: Motion library does not support virtual scroll
options.B: Animated enter/exit changes item height dynamically, causing layout shift and measurement issues
options.C: Virtual scroll and animations cannot coexist

cards.answer_text: Animated enter/exit changes item height dynamically, causing layout shift and measurement issues
cards.explanation: Animation that changes item height (collapse, expand, fade-out with height change) breaks virtualizer's measurement. Solution: overlay animations on top of virtualized container using absolute positioning, outside virtualized items.

cards.question: Which date library is tree-shakable and currently recommended for new apps?

options.B: date-fns

cards.answer_text: date-fns
cards.explanation: date-fns is tree-shakable (import only functions you use), TypeScript-native, immutable, and stable. Moment.js is deprecated. Day.js is mutable and not tree-shakable. Luxon is larger bundle.

cards.question: What is the recommended long-term strategy for date handling?

options.B: Use date-fns now with adapter; add Temporal adapter when native browser support ships (ES2025+)

cards.answer_text: Use date-fns now with adapter; add Temporal adapter when native browser support ships (ES2025+)
cards.explanation: Temporal is stage 4 and shipping in browsers 2025+. Start with date-fns adapter. When Temporal is available, add Temporal adapter implementation. Polyfill is too large for production.

cards.question: How should user timezone preference be stored?

options.B: IANA timezone string (e.g., Asia/Kolkata)
options.D: User's IP address — detect on every request

cards.answer_text: IANA timezone string (e.g., Asia/Kolkata)
cards.explanation: IANA timezone string accounts for DST changes and historical timezone changes. UTC offset does not handle DST. Detect via `Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone`.

cards.question: JavaScript Date stores milliseconds since epoch (UTC). What goes wrong when displayed directly?

options.B: toString(), getHours(), getDate() use local timezone, not UTC — same Date shows different times in different timezones
options.C: Date stores local time, not UTC

cards.answer_text: toString(), getHours(), getDate() use local timezone, not UTC — same Date shows different times in different timezones
cards.explanation: Date.getTime() is UTC. But Date.toString(), getHours(), getDate() use runtime local timezone. User in Tokyo sees different display than user in New York for same Date object. Use timezone-aware formatting.

cards.question: DateAdapter.format(date, 'yyyy-MM-dd') formats the same across date-fns, Day.js, and Temporal?

options.A: Yes — all use same pattern tokens
options.B: No — each library uses different pattern tokens (date-fns: yyyy-MM-dd, Day.js: YYYY-MM-DD, Temporal: Intl)

cards.answer_text: No — each library uses different pattern tokens (date-fns: yyyy-MM-dd, Day.js: YYYY-MM-DD, Temporal: Intl)
cards.explanation: date-fns uses Unicode token format (yyyy-MM-dd). Day.js uses Moment-style tokens (YYYY-MM-DD). Temporal uses Intl.DateTimeFormat options. Adapter hides these differences — consumers use adapter.format() with app-defined token format.

cards.question: Why is testing timezone logic difficult?

options.B: Intl and Temporal use system timezone — mocking timezone requires environment-level changes

cards.answer_text: Intl and Temporal use system timezone — mocking timezone requires environment-level changes
cards.explanation: Timezone formatting depends on system locale and timezone. Use libraries with injectable timezone (date-fns-tz formatInTimeZone, Temporal.TimeZone.from). Tests set explicit timezone in function calls, not system timezone.

cards.question: DatePickerAdapter maps between Date and picker library's date type. Why is this needed?

options.A: Date pickers accept different date objects (Dayjs, Moment, Luxon DateTime)
options.B: Date pickers do not accept JavaScript Date
options.D: All date pickers accept Date natively

cards.answer_text: Date pickers accept different date objects (Dayjs, Moment, Luxon DateTime)
cards.explanation: MUI X DatePicker accepts Dayjs or date-fns date. AntD DatePicker accepts Moment. React DatePicker accepts Date. DatePickerAdapter converts app's Date to picker-specific type.

cards.question: A user schedules a meeting for 3 PM in New York. What should be stored?

options.B: UTC timestamp + IANA timezone (America/New_York)

cards.answer_text: UTC timestamp + IANA timezone (America/New_York)
cards.explanation: Store UTC timestamp (absolute point in time) + IANA timezone (original context). Timezone is needed to display '3 PM ET' and to convert to other timezones. UTC alone loses original timezone context.

cards.question: DurationAdapter covers addDuration, formatDuration, humanized duration. What makes formatDuration tricky across libraries?

options.B: Libraries produce different output ('2 hours 30 minutes' vs '2h 30m' vs '150 minutes') — need locale-appropriate humanization

cards.answer_text: Libraries produce different output ('2 hours 30 minutes' vs '2h 30m' vs '150 minutes') — need locale-appropriate humanization
cards.explanation: formatDuration output varies by library and locale. date-fns formatDuration returns '2 hours 30 minutes'. Day.js humanize returns '2 hours'. Temporal with Intl returns locale-specific. Adapter standardizes output format.

cards.question: When would you NOT abstract date library behind adapter?

options.A: When app uses only Date.now() and simple toString()
options.B: When app is used globally with multiple timezones
options.C: When app performs complex calendar math
options.D: When app formats dates with user locale

cards.answer_text: When app uses only Date.now() and simple toString()
cards.explanation: If app only uses Date.now() and toISOString() (no formatting, no timezone, no calendar operations), abstraction is overhead. Add adapter when you call format, add, diff, or timezone functions.

cards.question: A library is used in one file, unlikely to change, and is a de-facto standard. What strategy?

options.B: Embed — direct dependency, no wrapper

cards.answer_text: Embed — direct dependency, no wrapper
cards.explanation: Embed when library is stable, single-use, and unlikely to change. Wrapping adds unnecessary indirection. Example: a utility like uuid or clsx.

cards.question: What migration strategy keeps both libraries loaded simultaneously?

options.B: Strangler Fig

cards.answer_text: Strangler Fig
cards.explanation: Strangler Fig gradually replaces library usage module by module while both libraries coexist behind feature flags. Big Bang replaces all at once.

cards.question: Adapter pattern solves which library versioning problem?

options.B: Different modules using different library versions — adapter provides uniform API regardless of underlying version

cards.answer_text: Different modules using different library versions — adapter provides uniform API regardless of underlying version
cards.explanation: Adapter hides library version behind common interface. Module A uses date-fns v2 adapter, Module B uses date-fns v3 adapter — both expose same DateAdapter. Version conflicts isolated inside adapter files.

cards.question: What is the primary risk of Big Bang migration?

options.B: Higher chance of regressions across entire app simultaneously

cards.answer_text: Higher chance of regressions across entire app simultaneously
cards.explanation: Big Bang replaces all at once. If migration has bugs, entire app is affected. Strangler Fig limits blast radius to one module at a time.

cards.question: Library audit reveals 5 date libraries in app. What action?

options.A: Keep all — each serves different purpose
options.B: Consolidate to one library; audit purpose of each, remove duplicates

cards.answer_text: Consolidate to one library; audit purpose of each, remove duplicates
cards.explanation: 5 date libraries indicate governance failure. Audit each library's usage. Keep only those with distinct, justified purpose (e.g., date-fns for general, Temporal for calendar math). Remove duplicates.

cards.question: Polyglot chart architecture: Recharts for dashboards, D3 for custom viz. When is this problematic?

options.B: When teams use Recharts for custom viz and D3 for dashboards — no clear boundaries
options.C: When both libraries are same bundle size

cards.answer_text: When teams use Recharts for custom viz and D3 for dashboards — no clear boundaries
cards.explanation: Polyglot works if each library has clear, documented context. Problems arise when boundaries blur — teams use wrong library for wrong task. Document: 'Recharts for standard business charts. D3 for custom visualizations only.'

cards.question: Strangler Fig requires both libraries in bundle. How to minimize bundle impact?

options.A: Do not use strangler fig — bundle will be too large
options.B: Dynamic import of new library when feature flag enabled; code-split old library removal
options.C: Load both libraries in one chunk

cards.answer_text: Dynamic import of new library when feature flag enabled; code-split old library removal
cards.explanation: Dynamic import (`await import('new-lib')`) ensures old and new libraries are not loaded together unless flag is toggled. When migration completes, remove old library dynamic import is removed.

cards.question: When does wrapping every library become an anti-pattern?

options.B: When wrapper adds no constraints, defaults, or transformations — thin wrapper indirection
options.C: When any module uses more than 3 wrappers

cards.answer_text: When wrapper adds no constraints, defaults, or transformations — thin wrapper indirection
cards.explanation: Thin wrapper that passes every prop through adds indirection without value. Wrap when you enforce constraints, set app defaults, or transform types. Otherwise, embed directly.

cards.question: How to estimate migration cost from library A to library B?

options.B: Count files importing library A × average effort per file (based on integration depth)
options.C: Count library A's API surface used vs library B's API — if similar, migration is free
options.D: Migration cost always equals bundle size difference

cards.answer_text: Count files importing library A × average effort per file (based on integration depth)
cards.explanation: Files × integration depth (simple import = 1h, complex component = 4h, deep lifecycle integration = 8h). API surface comparison helps but actual effort depends on integration complexity.

cards.question: Team evaluates embedding or abstracting a chart library for a new dashboard. The app currently has 2 charts in 1 file, expected to grow to 15 charts across 5 modules. What strategy?

options.A: Embed — only 2 charts now, abstraction is premature
options.B: Abstract — expected growth to 15 charts across multiple modules justifies adapter now
options.D: Wait until 10 chart files — then abstract

cards.answer_text: Abstract — expected growth to 15 charts across multiple modules justifies adapter now
cards.explanation: With expected growth to 15 charts across 5 modules, adding abstraction early pays off. Adding it later requires retrofitting. Write adapter now, use it for initial 2 charts. When migration happens, only adapter changes.

cards.question: Which Testing Library query should you use as the first preference?

options.B: getByText

cards.explanation: getByRole is the most accessible query. It finds elements by ARIA role and matches how assistive technology users navigate.

cards.question: What is the purpose of the wrapper option in Testing Library's render function?

options.B: Provide context providers like Router and QueryClient

cards.explanation: Wrapper allows injecting context providers around the tested component without repeating provider setup in every test.

cards.question: In Vitest setup with jsdom, why do we mock IntersectionObserver and ResizeObserver?

options.A: They are slow and slow down tests
options.B: They are not implemented in jsdom environment
options.C: They cause memory leaks in tests
options.D: Testing Library requires explicit mock declarations

cards.explanation: jsdom does not implement IntersectionObserver or ResizeObserver. Tests crash without stubs for these browser APIs.

cards.question: How does React 19 Concurrent Mode affect component tests?

options.A: It has no effect on tests
options.B: State updates may be batched, requiring act() wrapping
options.D: Testing Library throws warnings for concurrent rendering

cards.explanation: Concurrent Mode batches state updates. Tests wrapping interactions in act() ensures all updates flush before assertions.

cards.question: Which approach should be preferred for most component tests?

options.B: Behavior testing with user interactions

cards.explanation: Behavior testing validates actual user interactions and state changes, producing more resilient tests than snapshots.

cards.question: What happens when vi.mock is called at module level?

options.A: It is evaluated at function call time
options.B: It is hoisted to the top of the file before imports
options.C: It only applies within the current describe block
options.D: It requires explicit import path matching

cards.explanation: vi.mock is hoisted by Vitest's transform pipeline, replacing module imports before test code executes.

cards.question: Why should userEvent be preferred over fireEvent for interactions?

options.B: userEvent simulates full user interaction sequence including hover, focus, and keyboard events
options.C: fireEvent is deprecated in Testing Library v14

cards.explanation: userEvent dispatches the full chain of events (focus, keyDown, keyUp, click, blur) matching real browser behavior.

cards.question: When testing a component wrapping AG Grid, what is the recommended approach?

options.B: Mock AgGridReact and test the wrapper component's logic
options.C: Use end-to-end tests instead of unit tests

cards.explanation: AG Grid is a complex library with heavy DOM. Mocking the grid component isolates wrapper logic and keeps tests fast.

cards.question: What does React 19 StrictMode double-invocation mean for test cleanup?

options.A: Cleanup runs once regardless of StrictMode
options.B: Effects and cleanups run twice in development, requiring careful assertion count handling

cards.explanation: StrictMode double-invokes effects for detecting impure logic. Tests asserting on subscription counts must account for this.

cards.question: Which Vitest configuration disables retries for React Query in tests?

options.B: retry: false

cards.explanation: React Query retries failed queries by default. Setting retry: false in test QueryClient prevents unexpected retries from interfering with assertions.

cards.question: What problem does the twMerge function in the cn() helper solve?

options.B: It resolves conflicting Tailwind utility classes, keeping the last one
options.C: It combines separate CSS files into one bundle
options.D: It converts inline styles to Tailwind classes

cards.explanation: twMerge resolves specificity conflicts between Tailwind utilities (e.g., px-4 vs px-6), keeping the last defined class.

cards.question: Why is Tailwind fully compatible with React 19 Compiler?

options.B: Tailwind generates static class names at build time with zero runtime CSS-in-JS
options.C: React 19 Compiler only works with Tailwind
options.D: Tailwind uses runtime style injection for compatibility

cards.explanation: React 19 Compiler memoizes components automatically. Tailwind's static class names need no runtime style computation, so no migration effort is needed.

cards.question: What is the correct pattern for making a Button component accept custom styles from consumers?

options.B: Accept a className prop merged with cn()
options.C: Use CSS variables for all visual properties

cards.explanation: Accepting className and merging with cn() allows consumers to override or extend styles while preserving internal component styles.

cards.question: How do you configure Tailwind to support class-based dark mode toggling?

options.B: Set darkMode: 'class' in tailwind.config

cards.explanation: darkMode: 'class' enables toggling dark mode by adding/removing the 'dark' class on the HTML element. 'media' follows system preference.

cards.question: What is the advantage of mapping design tokens via CSS variables in tailwind.config?

options.A: CSS variables allow runtime theme switching without recompilation
options.B: CSS variables are faster than Tailwind utility classes
options.C: Tailwind only supports CSS variable tokens
options.D: CSS variables eliminate the need for Tailwind config

cards.explanation: CSS variables can be toggled at runtime for theme switching. Tailing config references them, so utility classes automatically reflect the current theme.

cards.question: Which Radix UI state attribute does Tailwind use for styling open/closed transitions?

options.B: data-state

cards.explanation: Radix UI components expose data-state attribute (e.g., data-[state=open], data-[state=closed]) for styling via Tailwind's data-* variant.

cards.question: What is the difference between Tailwind utility-first approach and CSS-in-JS?

options.A: Tailwind generates CSS at build time; CSS-in-JS generates styles at runtime or build time via JavaScript
options.B: They are the same approach with different syntax

cards.explanation: Tailwind uses PostCSS to generate CSS at build time. CSS-in-JS libraries may generate styles at runtime or use compile-time extraction.

cards.question: Which responsive breakpoint pattern creates a 4-column grid on desktop and 2-column on tablet?

options.B: grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-4

cards.explanation: In Tailwind mobile-first breakpoints, lg: overrides the default (mobile) grid. grid-cols-2 default becomes lg:grid-cols-4 on large screens.

cards.question: How should you handle repeated utility patterns that appear many times across components?

options.B: Extract into a reusable component with className prop
options.C: Add a custom utility class via @apply
options.D: Use inline styles for the repeated patterns

cards.explanation: Reusable components encapsulate utility patterns. className prop allows consumer customization while reducing duplication.

cards.question: What does the clsx function in the cn() helper do?

options.B: Conditionally joins class names together

cards.explanation: clsx handles conditional class name joining (e.g., clsx('base', condition && 'extra')) returning a single string.

cards.question: What does tRPC use to define both runtime validation and TypeScript types?

options.B: Zod schemas

cards.explanation: Zod schemas define runtime validation rules and TypeScript infers static types from them via z.infer().

cards.question: How does tRPC provide end-to-end type safety without code generation?

options.A: It generates TypeScript types at build time
options.B: TypeScript infers types from the server router definition shared with the client
options.D: It requires manual type definitions on both sides

cards.explanation: The AppRouter type is exported from the server. The client imports it as a generic parameter, and TypeScript infers all input/output types.

cards.question: Which tRPC pattern should you use for calling procedures from React 19 Server Components without an HTTP round-trip?

options.B: Server caller pattern with createCaller

cards.explanation: createCaller creates a server-side caller that invokes procedures directly in-process, bypassing HTTP. Used in Server Components.

cards.question: What is the purpose of tRPC middleware?

options.B: Inject cross-cutting concerns like auth, logging, and rate limiting

cards.explanation: Middleware intercepts procedure execution for cross-cutting logic. Multiple middleware compose in order, sharing context via next({ ctx: ... }).

cards.question: How do you invalidate cached queries after a mutation in tRPC?

options.B: Call utils.procedureName.invalidate() with the relevant input

cards.explanation: tRPC provides useUtils() returning helpers per procedure. Invalidate refetches the query with matching input keys.

cards.question: What does the Zod z.infer<typeof Schema> utility do?

options.B: Extracts the TypeScript type from a Zod schema definition
options.D: Converts Zod types to TypeScript interfaces

cards.explanation: z.infer extracts the static TypeScript type that a Zod schema validates against, enabling single-source-of-truth typing.

cards.question: Which React 19 hook is recommended for composing with tRPC mutations in progressive enhancement patterns?

options.B: useActionState

cards.explanation: useActionState handles form submission state and progressive enhancement. It pairs with tRPC mutateAsync for server mutations.

cards.question: What error code does tRPC use for unauthenticated requests?

options.B: FORBIDDEN

cards.explanation: tRPC maps TRPCError codes to HTTP status codes. UNAUTHORIZED maps to 401. FORBIDDEN (403) is for insufficient permissions despite authentication.

cards.question: How does tRPC integrate with TanStack React Query under the hood?

options.B: tRPC wraps React Query hooks, providing typed query/mutation hooks
options.D: They operate independently with no integration

cards.explanation: tRPC React Query adapter (createTRPCReact) generates typed hooks that internally use React Query's useQuery and useMutation.

cards.question: What happens when a tRPC middleware throws a TRPCError?

options.B: The error propagates to the client with the configured error formatter

cards.explanation: TRPCError propagates through the middleware chain to the client. The errorFormatter can add context like Zod validation errors.

cards.question: What does the priority prop on next/image do?

options.A: Sets the image as highest quality
options.B: Preloads the image with a <link rel=preload> tag
options.C: Prioritizes the image in the CDN queue

cards.explanation: priority adds a preload link in the document head, telling the browser to start loading the image immediately rather than waiting for discovery.

cards.question: Which Sharp pipeline step converts an image to WebP with quality control?

options.B: sharp(input).toFormat('webp', { quality: 80 })

cards.explanation: Sharp provides format-specific methods (.webp(), .avif(), .jpeg()) accepting an options object with quality and other format-specific settings.

cards.question: What is the purpose of blurDataURL in next/image?

options.B: Show a blurry placeholder while the full image loads, improving perceived performance
options.C: Reduce image quality for bandwidth savings

cards.explanation: blurDataURL is a tiny base64-encoded image shown as placeholder. placeholder='blur' must be set alongside blurDataURL for this effect.

cards.question: How should you use React 19 Server Components with image metadata?

options.A: Process image metadata in the Server Component and pass as props to the client component
options.B: Fetch metadata client-side with useEffect
options.C: Define metadata in a static JSON file
options.D: Use onLoad callback to capture metadata

cards.explanation: Server Components can read file system, process images with Sharp, and pass metadata (dimensions, blurDataURL) to client components as props.

cards.question: Which sizes attribute value serves full-viewport images on mobile and half-viewport on desktop?

options.B: (max-width: 768px) 100vw, 50vw

cards.explanation: The sizes attribute tells the browser what width the image will display at each breakpoint. Mobile gets 100vw, desktop gets 50vw.

cards.question: What does the 'withoutEnlargement: true' option in Sharp's resize do?

options.A: Crops the image instead of resizing
options.B: Prevents upscaling images smaller than the target dimensions
options.C: Enlarges images to fill the target size

cards.explanation: withoutEnlargement ensures small images aren't scaled up (which would degrade quality). The image stays at its original dimensions if smaller than target.

cards.question: What is the difference between WebP and AVIF formats?

options.A: WebP is smaller than AVIF at equal quality
options.B: AVIF offers ~20% better compression but lower browser support than WebP
options.C: WebP supports transparency, AVIF does not
options.D: AVIF is only supported in Chrome

cards.explanation: AVIF (AV1 Image File Format) provides better compression ratios than WebP but has lower browser support (~92% vs ~97%).

cards.question: What happens when you set fill on next/image without a parent with position: relative?

options.B: The image collapses to zero dimensions
options.D: The image uses intrinsic dimensions instead

cards.explanation: fill requires a parent with position: relative (or absolute/fixed) and defined dimensions. Without it, the image has no container to fill.

cards.question: Which native attribute defers image loading until near the viewport?

options.B: loading='lazy'

cards.explanation: loading='lazy' defers loading until the image approaches the viewport (browser-defined distance). decoding='async' is complementary but unrelated to loading timing.

cards.question: Why configure remotePatterns in next.config for image optimization?

options.A: It enables next/image to optimize images from external CDNs
options.B: It speeds up image download from external sources
options.D: It allows external images but disables optimization

cards.explanation: remotePatterns tells next/image which external hosts are safe to optimize. Optimized images are served through Next.js's optimization endpoint.

cards.question: Which component serves as the root container in @react-pdf/renderer?

options.B: Document

cards.explanation: Document is the root component that wraps one or more Page components. It receives global style and metadata props.

cards.question: What layout system does @react-pdf/renderer use for positioning elements?

options.B: Flexbox

cards.explanation: @react-pdf/renderer uses Flexbox for layout. Properties like flexDirection, justifyContent, alignItems, and flex work similarly to CSS Flexbox.

cards.question: How do you add custom fonts to a @react-pdf document?

options.A: Import font file directly in the component
options.B: Call Font.register with family name and source paths
options.C: Add font to a global CSS file

cards.explanation: Font.register registers fonts before rendering. It accepts a family name and array of font variants (weight, style, src).

cards.question: Which method converts a @react-pdf document to a downloadable Blob?

options.B: document.renderToBlob()

cards.explanation: pdf(document).toBlob() asynchronously renders the document and returns a Blob object suitable for download or URL creation.

cards.question: Why should useTransition be used for PDF generation button clicks?

options.B: It prevents UI freezes by marking PDF generation as a non-urgent state update
options.C: It adds animation to the download process
options.D: It automatically retries failed PDF generation

cards.explanation: useTransition marks the state update as non-urgent, allowing the browser to remain responsive during the PDF generation process.

cards.question: Which component is used to embed an image in a @react-pdf document?

options.B: Image

cards.explanation: The Image component accepts src (local path, remote URL, or base64 data URI), width, height, and objectFit props.

cards.question: What is the equivalent of a div element in @react-pdf/renderer?

options.B: View

cards.explanation: View is the generic layout container, equivalent to a div in HTML. Use it for grouping elements and applying layout styles.

cards.question: How should you handle large PDF documents in @react-pdf/renderer?

options.A: Generate the entire PDF in memory and download
options.B: Use toBuffer() for server-side streaming with appropriate Content-Length headers
options.C: Split the document into multiple smaller PDFs

cards.explanation: For large PDFs, use toBuffer() on the server and stream the response with Content-Length header for efficient delivery.

cards.question: How does React 19 Suspense integrate with @react-pdf document generation?

options.B: Use Suspense to show fallback UI while async data for PDF content is loading
options.D: Suspense replaces pdf().toBlob() for async generation

cards.explanation: Suspense wraps async data fetching for PDF content. The PDF component tree renders once data resolves, showing a fallback during loading.

cards.question: What happens if you use HTML elements like div or span inside a @react-pdf component?

options.B: They cause a runtime error or produce empty output
options.C: They are converted to View and Text components
options.D: They render correctly in all cases

cards.explanation: @react-pdf/renderer only understands its own components (Document, Page, View, Text, Image). HTML elements are not supported and cause errors or blank output.

cards.question: What is the correct unified pipeline order in react-markdown?

options.A: rehype plugins → remark plugins → React elements
options.B: remark plugins → rehype plugins → React elements
options.C: React elements → remark plugins → rehype plugins
options.D: rehype plugins → React elements → remark plugins

cards.explanation: Pipeline order: remarkParse (mdast) → remarkPlugins (transform mdast) → remarkRehype (mdast→hast) → rehypePlugins (transform hast) → render to React elements.

cards.question: What threat does rehype-sanitize protect against in react-markdown?

options.B: XSS attacks via embedded HTML and javascript: URLs
options.C: Memory leaks from large markdown files

cards.explanation: rehype-sanitize strips dangerous HTML elements and attributes. Prevents XSS vectors like <script> tags, javascript: URLs in links, onerror handlers, and other injection attacks.

cards.question: How would you add a Copy button to code blocks rendered by react-markdown?

options.A: Not possible — react-markdown does not support custom UI
options.B: Override the pre component in components prop with a wrapper containing Copy button
options.D: Inject Copy button via rehype plugin only

cards.explanation: Override pre element renderer via components prop. Wrap children with div containing Clipboard API button. Rehype plugin approach also works but component override is simpler.

cards.question: What does remark-gfm add that base react-markdown does not support?

options.B: Tables, strikethrough, task lists, URL autolinks

cards.explanation: GitHub Flavored Markdown adds tables (| col | col |), strikethrough (~~text~~), task lists (- [x]), URL autolinks, and footnotes. Base commonmark does not include these.

cards.question: How does React 19 Server Component rendering benefit markdown display?

options.A: Markdown can be edited by server admins at runtime
options.B: react-markdown bundle (~8KB) not sent to client — rendered to HTML on server
options.C: Server Components allow real-time markdown collaboration
options.D: Server Components automatically add syntax highlighting

cards.explanation: Server Component renders markdown on server, sends HTML. Client never downloads react-markdown or its plugin dependencies. Smaller bundle, faster initial render.

cards.question: What unist utility is commonly used to traverse and modify the markdown AST in custom remark plugins?

options.B: unist-util-visit

cards.explanation: unist-util-visit visits nodes matching a type. Pattern: visit(tree, 'heading', (node) => { ... }). Used in custom remark plugins to extract, modify, or annotate AST nodes.

cards.question: What happens when rehype-raw is used without rehype-sanitize?

options.B: Embedded HTML from markdown source renders without restriction — XSS risk

cards.explanation: rehype-raw parses HTML tags embedded in markdown into hast. Without rehype-sanitize, <script>, onerror, and javascript: URLs render. Always pair rehype-raw with rehype-sanitize.

cards.question: How does React 19 Suspense integrate with react-markdown for async content loading?

options.A: Wrap ReactMarkdown in Suspense — no additional setup
options.B: Use use() hook with fetch promise inside component, wrapped in Suspense boundary
options.C: ReactMarkdown has built-in async loading prop
options.D: Suspense only works with Server Components

cards.explanation: Fetch markdown as promise, pass to component using use() hook. Suspense boundary handles loading state. Pattern: const content = use(markdownPromise); return <ReactMarkdown>{content}</ReactMarkdown>.

cards.question: Which prop on ReactMarkdown overrides default HTML element rendering?

options.B: components

cards.explanation: components prop maps HTML tag names to custom React components. Example: components={{ pre: CustomPre, a: CustomLink, code: CustomCode }}.

cards.question: What is the purpose of remarkRehype in the unified pipeline?

options.B: Converts mdast (remark AST) to hast (rehype AST)
options.C: Adds syntax highlighting to code blocks

cards.explanation: remarkRehype bridges remark and rehype ecosystems. Converts mdast (Markdown AST) to hast (HTML AST) so rehype plugins can process the tree before React rendering.

cards.question: What is the purpose of Algolia's searchable attributes configuration?

options.A: Determines which attributes are returned in search results
options.B: Specifies which fields are searched when a user types a query
options.C: Defines the CSS class names for search UI components
options.D: Controls the order of results in the index

cards.explanation: Searchable attributes define which record fields Algolia scans for text matches. Only these fields contribute to relevance ranking. Non-searchable attributes are returned but not searched.

cards.question: What InstantSearch hook provides access to the current query and refine function?

options.B: useSearchBox

cards.explanation: useSearchBox returns { query, refine, clear }. query is current search string. refine updates query and triggers search. clear resets to empty.

cards.question: How does React 19 useTransition improve search input UX compared to debounce?

options.B: useTransition marks search refinement as low priority — input stays responsive, no fixed delay
options.C: useTransition replaces debounce with Promise-based throttling
options.D: useTransition and debounce serve the same purpose

cards.explanation: useTransition marks state update as non-urgent. React processes input update first, then search refinement. No magic number delay — adapts to device speed naturally.

cards.question: What is the correct way to scope search results by user in Algolia?

options.A: Filter on client side after receiving results
options.B: Use Secured API Keys with filters generated server-side
options.D: Add user_id field and filter in InstantSearch useRefinementList

cards.explanation: Generate Secured API Key on server with user-specific filter: client.generateSecuredApiKey(key, { filters: 'user_id:123' }). Client receives scoped key and cannot access other users' data.

cards.question: Which InstantSearch component renders matched text with <mark> tags?

options.B: <Highlight>

cards.explanation: Highlight component wraps matching query terms in <mark> tags for visual emphasis. Usage: <Highlight attribute=

cards.question: What is the difference between Highlight and Snippet in InstantSearch?
