options.B: Highlight marks matched words inline. Snippet truncates long text with ellipsis around matches
options.C: Snippet is for code blocks, Highlight is for text
options.D: Highlight works on client, Snippet works on server

cards.explanation: Highlight shows full attribute value with matched words in <mark>. Snippet truncates to relevant portion around matches (configure snippet size in Algolia dashboard).

cards.question: How would you build a fully custom search widget beyond prebuilt components?

options.A: Not possible — prebuilt components are required
options.B: Use hooks (useSearchBox, useHits, useRefinementList) and render custom JSX

cards.explanation: Hooks return { items, refine, currentRefinement } etc. Build any UI layout. Prebuilt components are optional convenience wrappers around hooks.

cards.question: What should you never expose on the client side in Algolia integration?

options.B: Admin API key

cards.explanation: Admin API key has full CRUD access to indices. Only search-only API key (limited to search queries) should be on client. Admin key used server-side for indexing.

cards.question: How do Server Components improve Algolia search page initial load?

options.A: Server Components cannot make API calls
options.B: Server Components fetch and render initial results on server — client sees results immediately without client-side Algolia request
options.D: Server Components cache Algolia responses in CDN

cards.explanation: Server Component fetches Algolia on server, renders HTML. Client hydrates with InstantSearch for subsequent interactive searches. Eliminates flash of empty results on page load.

cards.question: What does Algolia useRefinementList return for each facet value?

options.B: id, name, total, selected

cards.explanation: Each item: value (raw facet value for refine()), label (display text), count (matching result count), isRefined (currently selected). Pattern: { value, label, count, isRefined }.

cards.question: What does dependsOn: ['^build'] mean in turbo.json?

options.A: Run build after all tasks complete
options.B: Wait for upstream dependency builds before running this task
options.C: Build current package, then build dependencies

cards.explanation: ^ prefix indicates upstream dependencies. '^build' means this package's build waits for its dependencies' build tasks to complete first. Ensures compiled dependencies are available.

cards.question: What determines whether Turborepo returns a cache hit or executes a task?

options.A: Only the source file modification timestamps
options.B: Hash of task definition + input file hashes + global dependency hashes + env vars

cards.explanation: Cache key = hash(task definition, input glob hashes, globalDependencies hashes, env vars, lockfile). Any change invalidates cache. Consistent across machines.

cards.question: How does shared TypeScript config prevent configuration drift across packages?

options.A: Each package has independent config — no drift possible
options.B: Packages extend a base config from a shared package — changes propagate automatically
options.C: TypeScript CLI flags override per-package config

cards.explanation: Shared config package (e.g., config-typescript/base.json) defines base compiler options. Consumer tsconfig uses extends: 'config-typescript/nextjs.json'. Updating shared config updates all consumers.

cards.question: What is the purpose of remote caching in Turborepo?

options.A: Store build artifacts on users local machine
options.B: Share cached build outputs across CI machines and developer machines
options.D: Cache npm packages for faster installs

cards.explanation: Remote cache stores build outputs in cloud (Vercel, S3). CI pipeline and developers share cache. PR build skips if main branch already built same inputs.

cards.question: What is the correct way for a library package to declare React dependency in a monorepo?

options.A: Direct dependency on react in library's package.json
options.B: Peer dependency with supported version range
options.C: No declaration — React is provided globally

cards.explanation: Peer dependency: 'react': '^18.0.0 || ^19.0.0'. Ensures consumer app controls React version. Monorepo's single React instance shared across all packages. Avoids duplicate React installation.

cards.question: What does outputs: [] in a turbo.json pipeline task mean?

options.A: Task outputs are stored in default cache directory
options.B: Task has no cacheable outputs — always executes
options.C: Outputs are deleted after task completes

cards.explanation: Empty outputs array means task produces no cacheable artifacts. Examples: lint, typecheck, test. These tasks always execute because caching them provides no benefit.

cards.question: How to run a Turborepo task for only one workspace?

options.B: turbo run build --filter=@myorg/ui

cards.explanation: --filter flag targets specific workspace. Example: turbo run build --filter=@myorg/ui. Builds only that package and its dependencies. Other workspaces unaffected.

cards.question: What is the recommended strategy for adopting React 19 in a monorepo with existing React 18 packages?

options.B: Create canary versions of packages, canary app workspace, dual peer dependency support
options.C: Stay on React 18 until all dependencies support React 19
options.D: Use React 18 and React 19 in same bundle

cards.explanation: Gradual rollout: canary package variants with React 19, separate canary app workspace, peer dependency range '^18.0.0 || ^19.0.0'. Test in CI with both versions before full migration.

cards.question: What file defines Turborepo's task graph, dependencies, caching, and outputs?

options.B: turbo.json

cards.explanation: turbo.json at project root defines pipeline (tasks, dependsOn, outputs, inputs, env), globalDependencies, remoteCache configuration. Schema at https://turbo.build/schema.json.

cards.question: What does globalDependencies: ['.env'] in turbo.json achieve?

options.A: Makes .env available to all task processes
options.B: Includes .env hash in cache key — cache invalidates when .env changes
options.C: Copies .env to each workspace during build
options.D: Validates .env file exists before running tasks

cards.explanation: globalDependencies lists files whose content contributes to cache key for all tasks. .env changes invalidate all caches. Prevents stale builds when environment variables change.

cards.question: What is the preferred locator strategy in Playwright?

options.B: XPath for complex DOM queries
options.C: Accessible locators (getByRole, getByLabel) — resilient to DOM changes

cards.explanation: getByRole and getByLabel match how users and assistive tech interact. Resilient to DOM structure changes. CSS/XPath tied to implementation. Prefer role/label, then text, then test-id.

cards.question: What does page.route() intercept in Playwright?

options.B: Network requests — allows mocking, blocking, or modifying responses

cards.explanation: page.route() intercepts HTTP requests matching URL pattern. Supports fulfill (mock response), abort (block), fetch (pass through and modify). Used for offline testing, error states, slow responses.

cards.question: How does Playwright handle dynamic content loading without explicit waits?

options.A: Assertions auto-retry until condition met or timeout — no manual wait/sleep
options.B: Use page.waitForTimeout() for all dynamic content
options.C: Playwright does not support dynamic content
options.D: CSS animations must be disabled for assertions to work

cards.explanation: Playwright assertions (toHaveText, toBeVisible) auto-retry with configurable timeout. No manual sleep/wait. Waits disappear with each retry — test stays as fast as content loads.

cards.question: What is the purpose of BrowserContext in Playwright?

options.B: Isolated session with own cookies, localStorage, and storageState
options.C: Configuration file for browser launch options

cards.explanation: BrowserContext is isolated browser session. Each context has separate cookies, localStorage, and cache. Enables parallel tests with different auth states. storageState persists context.

cards.question: What does toHaveScreenshot() compare against?

options.B: Stored baseline screenshot in snapshot directory

cards.explanation: toHaveScreenshot compares current render against stored baseline image in snapshotDir (default __snapshots__/). maxDiffPixelRatio allows small tolerance. Fails if diff exceeds threshold.

cards.question: How should React 19 Server Components be identified in E2E tests?

options.A: Server Components cannot be tested in E2E
options.B: Use data attributes (data-server-rendered) or locator text content — HTML present in initial response
options.C: Test only client components wrapping Server Components

cards.explanation: Server Components render HTML server-side, visible in page source. Use data attributes for resilience or text-based assertions. getByRole/getByText work on server-rendered content.

cards.question: What is the Page Object Model pattern in Playwright?

options.A: Pattern where each test file represents one page
options.B: Class that encapsulates page interactions and locators — reusable across tests
options.C: Page is mocked object in test setup
options.D: Page rendered as object in snapshot

cards.explanation: Page Object Model creates class per page (LoginPage, CheckoutPage). Exposes locators and methods (login(), fillShippingInfo()). Reduces duplication when same interaction used across tests.

cards.question: How should visual regression tests handle components with dynamic data?

options.A: Do not use visual regression for dynamic components
options.B: Mock API data with page.route() before navigation — deterministic snapshots

cards.explanation: Mock API responses with page.route() to control rendered data. Same mock each run produces identical snapshots. Works for any component fetching data.

cards.question: What Playwright feature records execution details for debugging failures?

options.B: Trace Viewer — captures action log, screenshots, network, console, and DOM snapshots

cards.explanation: Trace records full execution: timeline, DOM snapshots before/after each action, network requests, console logs. Opened via npx playwright show-trace. Enabled with trace: 'on' or 'on-first-retry'.

cards.question: What is the purpose of test.use({ storageState: 'auth/user.json' }) in Playwright?

options.A: Stores test results in JSON file
options.B: Reuses pre-authenticated browser state — skips login step for subsequent tests
options.D: Caches API responses for faster tests

cards.explanation: storageState loads saved cookies and localStorage into BrowserContext. Tests start already authenticated. Commonly created by first test (login → save context) then reused by remaining suite.

cards.question: What is the purpose of typePolicies in Apollo Client's InMemoryCache?

options.A: Defines GraphQL schema types for validation
options.B: Customizes cache behavior: keyFields for cache IDs, merge strategies for field updates
options.C: Configures HTTP headers for GraphQL requests
options.D: Generates TypeScript types from GraphQL schema

cards.explanation: typePolicies configure per-type cache behavior. keyFields defines custom cache ID (default __typename:id). merge controls how incoming data merges with cached data (critical for pagination). read computes derived fields.

cards.question: How does Apollo Client's normalized cache differ from React Query's document cache?

options.A: No difference — both store query responses as-is
options.B: Normalized cache stores each entity once by ID. Document cache stores each query response separately
options.C: Apollo stores cache in IndexedDB. React Query stores in memory
options.D: Normalized cache is slower but more accurate

cards.explanation: Normalized cache: entities normalized by ID (User:123 stored once). Two queries returning same user update single cache entry. Document cache: each query response cached as blob — same user data duplicated across queries.

cards.question: What Apollo Client hook enables Suspense integration in React 19?

options.B: useSuspenseQuery — throws promise to Suspense boundary

cards.explanation: useSuspenseQuery throws promise during fetch, caught by parent Suspense boundary. Component renders only when data available. No loading state in component.

cards.question: How do you handle UNAUTHENTICATED errors globally in Apollo Client?

options.A: Catch error in each useQuery's onError callback
options.B: Use onError link to intercept errors and redirect to login

cards.explanation: onError link intercepts all GraphQL and network errors. Check err.extensions.code === 'UNAUTHENTICATED' in onError link handler. Redirect to login page or refresh token.

cards.question: What is the correct way to append paginated data in Apollo cache?

options.A: Cache appends automatically — no configuration needed
options.B: Define merge function in typePolicies that combines existing and incoming arrays
options.D: Set paginated: true in query options

cards.explanation: typePolicies field merge function controls list merging. Example: merge(existing = [], incoming) => [...existing, ...incoming]. Without custom merge, Apollo replaces existing data with incoming.

cards.question: What is the purpose of fragment composition in Apollo Client?

options.A: Splits large queries into smaller network requests
options.B: Reusable field selections shared across queries — single source of truth for data shape
options.C: Fragments are deprecated in Apollo Client v4
options.D: Fragments improve query performance by reducing payload

cards.explanation: Fragments define reusable field sets. Query includes fragment with ...spread syntax. Change fragment definition → all queries using it update. Improves maintainability of GraphQL document colocation.

cards.question: When should you choose React Query over Apollo Client?

options.A: Apollo Client is always better for any React app
options.B: REST or mixed REST/GraphQL APIs — React Query works with any async function
options.C: React Query only for REST APIs
options.D: React Query replaces Apollo Client for GraphQL too

cards.explanation: React Query is protocol-agnostic. Works with any async function (REST, GraphQL, tRPC). Apollo Client is GraphQL-only. Use React Query for mixed or non-GraphQL APIs. Use Apollo Client for GraphQL-heavy apps.

cards.question: How does Apollo Client handle subscriptions (real-time data)?

options.B: useSubscription hook with WebSocket link — receives pushed data from server
options.C: Subscriptions require external library — not built into Apollo Client
options.D: Subscriptions work only with Apollo Server

cards.explanation: useSubscription listens to GraphQL subscription via WebSocket. Server pushes data when events occur. Requires GraphQLWsLink or WebSocketLink for transport. Built-in, no external library needed.

cards.question: What does cache.modify() do in Apollo Client?

options.B: Modifies specific cached field values without refetch — used after mutations
options.D: Updates HTTP headers for next query

cards.explanation: cache.modify() updates specific cached fields directly. Pattern: cache.modify({ id: cache.identify(user), fields: { name: () => newName } }). Avoids refetch when mutation result already known.

cards.question: How does React 19 useTransition improve pagination UX with Apollo Client?

options.B: Marks page state change as low priority — stale content visible during fetch, no loading flash
options.D: useTransition disables pagination buttons during fetch

cards.explanation: useTransition wraps page state update as non-urgent. React renders stale page content while next page loads. Suspense handles loading. User sees previous page until next page ready.

cards.question: What is the difference between Howl and Howler in Howler.js?

options.A: Howl is global singleton; Howler manages individual instances
options.B: Howl manages individual audio instances; Howler is the global singleton for mute/volume/spatial
options.D: Howl handles sprites; Howler handles non-sprite audio

cards.explanation: Howl creates individual audio instances with playback control. Howler provides global state: mute, volume, listener position for spatial audio, and codec detection.

cards.question: How are audio sprites defined in Howler.js?

options.A: Array of file paths, played in sequence
options.B: Object mapping sprite names to [offsetMs, durationMs] arrays
options.C: Separate JSON manifest file linked in constructor
options.D: CSS animation timing applied to audio element

cards.explanation: Sprite definition is Record<string, [number, number]> where each entry maps name to [offsetMs, durationMs] within a single audio file.

cards.question: Which method signature correctly seeks a specific sound instance?

options.B: howl.seek(5, soundId)

cards.explanation: Howl.seek(seconds, soundId) seeks specific playback instance. Without soundId, seeks first/default instance. Howler has no seek method.

cards.question: What is the purpose of providing multiple formats in Howl src array?

options.A: Play all formats simultaneously for redundancy
options.B: Howler picks first playable format based on browser codec support
options.C: Increases audio quality by merging codecs
options.D: Required for sprite sheets to work

cards.explanation: Howler detects browser codec support and selects first playable entry from src array. Pattern: [webm, ogg, mp3] for optimal coverage.

cards.question: How does the React 19 ref-as-prop pattern work with Howler.js?

options.A: Ref holds Howl instance created inside effect
options.B: Ref exposes imperative audio control methods (play, pause, seek) to parent
options.C: Ref replaces useState for audio state
options.D: Ref stores audio buffer for visualizer

cards.explanation: Ref-as-prop in React 19 passes ref to child, which assigns handle object with play/pause/seek/volume methods. Parent calls audio.handle.play() imperatively.

cards.question: Why use useTransition when triggering audio sprite playback?

options.B: Prevents UI jank during rapid section switching by marking state update as low priority

cards.explanation: useTransition marks state update as non-urgent, keeping UI responsive during rapid playlist navigation. Audio playback itself is imperative and unaffected by React render.

cards.question: What happens if seek is called on a sprite-based Howl instance?

options.A: Seeks to position within current sprite
options.B: Seeks to position within full audio file, not relative to sprite
options.C: Throws error — seek not supported with sprites

cards.explanation: Seek position is relative to full audio buffer, not sprite. To control sprite playback position, restart the sprite or adjust sprite offset definition.

cards.question: Which pattern prevents memory leaks when using Howler.js in React components?

options.B: Set howl reference to null on unmount
options.C: Use ref instead of state to store Howl
options.D: Wrap Howl in useMemo with empty deps

cards.explanation: Howl allocates AudioContext resources. useEffect cleanup must call howl.unload() to release buffers and disconnect audio graph. Without unload, memory grows per mount.

cards.question: How does Howler.js handle browser autoplay restrictions?

options.B: Must resume AudioContext via Howler.ctx.resume() after user gesture
options.C: Schedules play for after page load
options.D: Always autoplays in Web Worker context

cards.explanation: Browsers suspend AudioContext until user gesture. Call Howler.ctx.resume() inside click/submit handler to resume audio processing. Pattern: one-time gesture listener at app root.

cards.question: When should the Sound Pool pattern be used with Howler.js?

options.A: Always — pool is default best practice
options.B: When same sound played repeatedly and overlapping (e.g., UI clicks, game sfx)
options.D: Pool replaces sprite sheet for large audio files

cards.explanation: Sound Pool pre-allocates multiple Howl instances and rotates play() calls. Prevents allocation overhead for rapid overlapping sounds like UI clicks or game events. Not needed for sequential playback.

cards.question: What is the purpose of Vidstack provider abstraction?

options.A: Provides DRM decryption for all video formats
options.B: Normalizes Html5Video, HLS, YouTube, and Vimeo APIs behind common interface
options.C: Replaces HTMLVideoElement entirely with WebGL rendering
options.D: Compresses video files on the client

cards.explanation: Provider abstraction lets developers swap between video sources (mp4, HLS, YouTube, Vimeo) without changing player UI or control logic. Each provider implements MediaProviderAdapter.

cards.question: Which Vidstack components are required for a minimal player?

options.B: MediaPlayer and MediaOutlet

cards.explanation: MediaPlayer orchestrates state and tracks. MediaOutlet renders the underlying video element/iframe. Controls are optional and slot into MediaControls.

cards.question: How does Vidstack determine which provider to use?

options.B: Auto-detection from src URL extension or type

cards.explanation: Vidstack inspects src extension (.mp4, .m3u8) or URL pattern (youtube.com, vimeo.com) to select appropriate provider. Manual override via type prop if needed.

cards.question: How does Vidstack integrate hls.js for HLS streaming?

options.B: Separate @vidstack/hls package with HlsProvider registration
options.C: Manual hls.js instance passed via ref
options.D: HLS support only available in premium tier

cards.explanation: @vidstack/hls exports HlsProvider registered via defineCustomElement. hlsConfig prop passes options like maxBufferLength, startLevel, abrEwmaDefaultEstimate.

cards.question: What is the slot system in Vidstack UI components?

options.B: Named slots replacing default icons (play/pause/fullscreen) with custom SVGs
options.C: Slot machine animation for loading states

cards.explanation: Slot attribute (slot='play', slot='pause', slot='on', slot='off') on child elements replaces default icons while preserving accessibility and behavior.

cards.question: Why use useTransition for timeupdate event handlers in Vidstack?

options.B: Prevents UI jank by marking progress setState as non-urgent during 4-60Hz events

cards.explanation: timeupdate fires at high frequency. useTransition marks React state update as low priority to avoid frame drops during video playback. UI stays responsive.

cards.question: Which track types does Vidstack support?

options.B: Subtitles, chapters, and quality levels

cards.explanation: Vidstack supports subtitles (VTT), chapter markers (VTT), and quality level tracks rendered via MediaQualitySlider. Track elements declared as children of MediaPlayer.

cards.question: How does Vidstack handle responsive video across screen sizes?

options.B: aspectRatio prop for ratio locking and src array with media conditions for art direction

cards.explanation: aspectRatio prop locks player dimensions. src array with media condition strings enables art-direction responsive video (different source per breakpoint).

cards.question: What is the benefit of React Compiler with Vidstack media event handlers?

options.A: Compiler converts media events to Web Workers
options.B: Auto-memoizes event handlers, preventing unnecessary re-renders from high-frequency media events
options.C: Compiler generates native video element code
options.D: No benefit — media events are not reactive

cards.explanation: React Compiler automatically memoizes callbacks and components. Vidstack onTimeUpdate fires 4-60 times/second — Compiler prevents these from causing cascading re-renders.

cards.question: When would you NOT use Vidstack and instead use raw HTMLVideoElement?

options.A: Simple single-MP4 playback without controls customization
options.B: HLS live streaming with quality selector

cards.explanation: Vidstack adds bundle size for provider abstraction. For a single MP4 with default controls, bare <video> element with controls attribute is simpler and lighter.

cards.question: What is the difference between QRCodeCanvas and QRCodeSVG in react-qrcode?

options.A: Canvas supports color; SVG is monochrome
options.B: Canvas renders to canvas element (good for export); SVG renders scalable vector (good for inline display)
options.C: SVG is faster; Canvas supports more error levels
options.D: They are identical — name only differs for SSR compatibility

cards.explanation: QRCodeCanvas renders to <canvas> — use for export (toDataURL, toBlob). QRCodeSVG renders to <svg> — use for inline display, copy, embedding, scaling.

cards.question: Which error correction level should be used when overlaying a logo on a QR code?

options.B: M (15% recovery)
options.D: Error correction has no effect on logo overlays

cards.explanation: Logo covers 15-25% of QR modules. Need Q (25%) or H (30%) recovery to ensure scan succeeds. L or M will fail with typical logo sizes.

cards.question: What does the excate prop do in react-qrcode imageSettings?

options.B: Removes QR modules behind logo to prevent scan interference
options.C: Excavates background color around QR code

cards.explanation: excate=true tells renderer to clear QR data modules in logo area. Without this, logo overlay creates contrast issues that degrade scanning.

cards.question: Which barcode format is appropriate for retail product labeling?

options.B: EAN-13

cards.explanation: EAN-13 is the international retail product barcode standard. CODE128 is general alphanumeric. CODE39 is logistics. DataMatrix is for small electronics.

cards.question: Why use virtual scrolling for batch QR code generation?

options.A: Virtual scrolling increases QR generation speed
options.B: Prevents DOM thrashing by only rendering visible QR codes
options.C: Required for jsbarcode to work in batches

cards.explanation: Rendering hundreds of QR/barcode DOM nodes simultaneously causes layout thrashing and jank. Virtual scroll renders only visible items, recycling DOM nodes.

cards.question: How does the React 19 ref-as-prop pattern enable QR export to PNG?

options.A: Ref holds QR value as string
options.B: Ref provides access to canvas element for toDataURL/toBlob calls
options.C: Ref triggers automatic download on mount

cards.explanation: Canvas ref via ref-as-prop gives imperative access to underlying <canvas>. Call canvas.toDataURL('image/png') or canvas.toBlob() to trigger PNG download.

cards.question: What is the advantage of using React 19 Server Components for QR codes?

options.B: Zero JS shipped to client — QR rendered to SVG string at build/request time
options.C: Higher error correction available on server
options.D: Server can generate animated QR codes

cards.explanation: Server Components pre-render QR to SVG string deterministically. No QR library JS sent to client. Wrap in Suspense for streaming delivery.

cards.question: Which jsbarcode format supports full ASCII character set?

options.B: UPC-A

cards.explanation: CODE128 supports full ASCII character set (alphanumeric + symbols) with variable length. EAN-13/UPC-A are fixed-length numeric. ITF is numeric only.

cards.question: When would you prefer react-qrcode over jsbarcode for generating QR codes?

options.A: Always — react-qrcode supports all barcode formats
options.B: For QR codes specifically — better error correction, customization, and React integration
options.D: They both generate identical QR output

cards.explanation: react-qrcode specializes in QR codes with better customization, error correction levels, and React component API. jsbarcode supports QR but is focused on linear barcodes.

cards.question: What is the correct approach for high-resolution QR export suitable for print?

options.B: Scale canvas element using drawImage with resolution multiplier before toDataURL
options.D: Export as JPEG for smaller file size

cards.explanation: Screen canvas resolution is limited. For print, draw canvas to offscreen canvas at 4x scale via drawImage with scale transform, then export via toDataURL at quality 1.

cards.question: What are the three core components of react-resizable-panels?

options.B: PanelGroup, Panel, PanelResizeHandle

cards.explanation: PanelGroup wraps the layout (direction). Panel defines resizable regions with min/max/collapse. PanelResizeHandle is the draggable separator between panels.

cards.question: What is the difference between controlled and uncontrolled Panel?

options.A: Controlled uses size prop with onLayout; uncontrolled uses defaultSize
options.B: Controlled panels are not resizable by users

cards.explanation: Controlled mode: parent manages sizes via size prop and receives updates via onLayout. Uncontrolled mode: panel manages own size via defaultSize without external state.

cards.question: What happens if panel sizes total exceeds 100%?

options.B: Sizes are normalized proportionally to sum to 100%

cards.explanation: PanelGroup auto-normalizes sizes. If panels sum > 100%, each scales down proportionally. Use onLayout output (always normalized) for persistence.

cards.question: How to persist panel layout across sessions?

options.B: Use onLayout callback to save sizes array to localStorage; restore via size prop
options.C: Panels persist automatically via React state
options.D: Store panel refs in global context

cards.explanation: onLayout fires number[] of percentage sizes. Save to localStorage. On mount, read saved array and pass as size prop to each Panel in controlled mode.

cards.question: Why use useTransition when persisting panel layout to localStorage?

options.B: Debounces high-frequency onLayout calls during drag, preventing main thread blocking
options.C: Transition enables undo of layout changes
options.D: Layout persistence is async by default

cards.explanation: onLayout fires during every pixel of drag at high frequency. useTransition marks localStorage write as low priority, keeping resize responsive.

cards.question: How are Suspense boundaries used with resizable panels?

options.A: Suspense wraps PanelGroup for loading state
options.B: Each Panel wraps its content in Suspense for lazy-loaded panel content
options.C: PanelResizeHandle supports Suspense for resize animations

cards.explanation: Each Panel wraps its content in <Suspense> so lazy-loaded panel sections stream independently. One panel's loading state does not block adjacent panels.

cards.question: What keyboard keys resize panels by default?

options.B: Arrow keys (left/right/up/down), Home/End, Enter

cards.explanation: Arrow keys adjust size incrementally. Home/End minimize/maximize panel. Enter toggles collapse. Enabled without extra configuration.

cards.question: How to create a collapsible sidebar that hides completely?

options.A: Set collapsible=true and collapsedSize=0 on Panel
options.B: Use visibility:hidden CSS class

cards.explanation: collapsible=true enables collapse. collapsedSize=0 hides panel completely. onCollapse/onExpand callbacks notify parent of state change.

cards.question: How does RTL support work in react-resizable-panels?

options.B: dir='rtl' prop on PanelGroup reverses handle resize direction
options.C: CSS direction property on parent element

cards.explanation: dir='rtl' on PanelGroup reverses resize behavior — dragging handle left increases right panel. Arrow keys also reverse accordingly.

cards.question: How to programmatically trigger panel resize via React 19 ref?

options.A: Panels expose resize(percentage) and collapse()/expand() methods on ref
options.B: Set size prop to new value — ref not needed for imperative control
options.C: Dispatch custom DOM event on panel element

cards.explanation: Panels expose imperative API via ref: resize(percentage), collapse(), expand(). Pass ref as prop (React 19 pattern) for parent-controlled imperative operations.

cards.question: What is the bundle size and dependency count of react-colorful?

options.B: 3KB gzipped, zero dependencies

cards.explanation: react-colorful is 3KB gzipped with zero external dependencies. This makes it the smallest React color picker library.

cards.question: Which component would you use for picking colors with alpha channel support?

options.B: HexAlphaColorPicker or RgbaStringColorPicker

cards.explanation: HexAlphaColorPicker outputs #rrggbbaa. RgbaStringColorPicker outputs rgba(r, g, b, a). Standard HexColorPicker does not support alpha.

cards.question: How does react-colorful support custom theming?

options.B: CSS custom properties (--r, --g, --b, --h, --s, --l) reflecting current color
options.C: Inline style overrides via defaultStyles prop

cards.explanation: react-colorful exposes CSS variables (--r, --g, --b, --h, --s, --l) that update live with current color. Use them in CSS selectors on picker class.

cards.question: Why must react-colorful components have a Client boundary ('use client')?

options.A: Library has not been updated for React 19
options.B: Picker requires browser APIs (pointer events, DOM measurements) for drag interaction
options.C: Server Components cannot import any npm packages
options.D: CSS variables only work in browser

cards.explanation: Color picker needs pointer/mouse/touch events and DOM element measurements for saturation square and hue slider interaction. Server Components have no browser API access.

cards.question: What keyboard interactions does react-colorful support?

options.B: Arrow keys for hue and saturation adjustment, Tab between controls
options.C: Number input fields for exact color values

cards.explanation: Arrow keys adjust hue (left/right) and saturation (up/down). Tab moves focus between saturation area and hue slider. ARIA labels on interactive elements.

cards.question: How does React Compiler optimize color picker onChange handlers?

options.A: Compiler skips re-render for all color changes
options.B: Auto-memoizes onChange callback, preventing unnecessary re-renders during drag
options.C: Compiler moves color calculations to Web Worker

cards.explanation: onChange fires many times per second during color picker drag. React Compiler automatically memoizes the handler and derived values, reducing re-render work.

cards.question: Which approach outputs color as RGB object from react-colorful?

options.B: RgbColorPicker component outputs { r, g, b }

cards.explanation: RgbColorPicker directly outputs { r, g, b }. HexColorPicker output can be converted via hexToRgb utility. HslColorPicker output can be converted via hslToRgb.

cards.question: What design pattern is illustrated by the ThemeColorPicker with presets?

options.A: Facade — simplify complex color model API
options.B: Wrapper — compose preset grid and custom picker with unified interface
options.C: Adapter — convert hex to RGB behind the scenes
options.D: Strategy — swap picker variant at runtime

cards.explanation: Wrapper composes preset selection and custom picker behind single ThemeColorPicker component with consistent value/onChange API. Consumers do not manage tab state.

cards.question: What is the react-colorful approach to color format conversion?

options.A: Built-in convert() method on picker instance
options.B: Library does not include conversion utilities — use small custom helpers
options.C: Conversion via external color-convert peer dependency
options.D: Automatic conversion based on output component variant

cards.explanation: react-colorful provides pickers for each format but no cross-format conversion. Write small hexToRgb, rgbToHsl helpers (~10 lines each) when needed.

cards.question: When should you choose react-colorful over a native input type='color'?

options.B: When you need custom styling, preset colors, alpha channel, or exact color model control
options.D: When bundle size is not a concern

cards.explanation: Native input type='color' is 0KB but limited: no alpha, no HSL/HSV, no custom styling, small palette preview. react-colorful excels when app needs branded picker or specific color model.

cards.question: What security requirement must the page satisfy for navigator.clipboard.writeText to work?

options.B: Page must be loaded over HTTP
options.C: Clipboard API requires no security constraints

cards.explanation: navigator.clipboard API requires a secure context (HTTPS or localhost). Insecure HTTP contexts throw an error.

cards.question: Which MIME types should be included when copying rich content (e.g., a table) to clipboard?

options.B: text/html and text/plain

cards.explanation: Rich content copy should include both text/html (formatted, for rich editors) and text/plain (fallback, for plain text targets).

cards.question: What is the recommended approach for reading clipboard contents in a secure context?

options.B: document.execCommand('paste')

cards.explanation: navigator.clipboard.readText() is the modern async API for reading clipboard text. execCommand is deprecated. clipboardData on events is for paste handling, not programmatic read.

cards.question: In React 19, why must clipboard hooks use 'use client'?

options.A: Clipboard API reads are slow and block Server Components
options.B: navigator.clipboard is only available in browser environments, not on the server
options.D: Clipboard API requires useState which is not available on server

cards.explanation: navigator is a browser global. Server Components execute on the server where navigator does not exist. 'use client' marks the boundary.

cards.question: What problem does paste sanitization primarily solve beyond security?

options.A: Reduces paste latency by skipping formatting
options.B: Prevents layout corruption from Word/Excel inline styles

cards.explanation: Word and Excel paste includes absolute positioning, mso-* styles, and colgroup markup that clash with app CSS. Sanitization normalizes to semantic HTML.

cards.question: Which React 19 feature is used in the useCopyToClipboard hook for non-urgent state updates (copy feedback)?

options.B: useTransition

cards.explanation: useTransition marks the copied state update as low-priority, preventing copy feedback from blocking urgent UI updates like typing.

cards.question: What is the legacy fallback for copying text when navigator.clipboard.writeText is unavailable?

options.B: document.execCommand('copy') with a hidden textarea
options.C: navigator.clipboard.writeText() is available in all browsers

cards.explanation: document.execCommand('copy') with a textarea.select() is the deprecated but widely supported fallback for legacy browsers.

cards.question: What permission status does clipboard-read typically start as in modern browsers?

options.B: denied

cards.explanation: clipboard-read permission starts as 'prompt' and triggers a browser permission dialog. clipboard-write is auto-granted in secure contexts.

cards.question: Which of the following triggers is NOT sufficient for clipboard API access?

options.B: keydown event handler

cards.explanation: Clipboard API requires a user gesture (click, keydown, touchstart). DOMContentLoaded fires automatically without user interaction.

cards.question: When the React Compiler is enabled, what happens to the copy callback in useCopyToClipboard?

options.A: It becomes a Server Action automatically
options.B: It is automatically memoized, removing the need for useCallback wrapping
options.C: It is transformed to use execCommand instead of navigator.clipboard
options.D: It is stripped from the bundle because clipboard code is server-only

cards.explanation: React Compiler auto-memoizes functions and values, so the useCallback wrapper around the copy function is no longer necessary.

cards.question: Which of the following best describes usehooks-ts compared to react-use?

options.B: Zero dependencies, tree-shakeable, TypeScript-first
options.D: Requires lodash as a peer dependency

cards.explanation: usehooks-ts has zero external dependencies, each hook is tree-shakeable, and the entire library is written in TypeScript.

cards.question: What does useLocalStorage return during SSR (server-side rendering)?

options.A: Throws an error because localStorage is not available
options.B: The default value passed to the hook
options.D: Reads from a server-side cookie store

cards.explanation: useLocalStorage returns the initial default value during SSR because window.localStorage does not exist on the server.

cards.question: In React 19, what types can useEventListener accept as its target parameter?

options.B: ref object, window, document, or HTMLElement

cards.explanation: React 19 allows ref as prop, so useEventListener accepts ref objects directly, along with window, document, and HTMLElement.

cards.question: What is the key difference between useDebounce from usehooks-ts and lodash.debounce?

options.B: useDebounce returns a debounced value; lodash.debounce returns a debounced function
options.C: useDebounce returns a debounced function; lodash.debounce returns a debounced value
options.D: useDebounce uses setTimeout; lodash.debounce uses requestAnimationFrame

cards.explanation: useDebounce is declarative: it returns the value after a delay. lodash.debounce is imperative: it wraps a function to delay invocations.

cards.question: When useMediaQuery returns false during SSR, what technique ensures the correct value is shown post-hydration?

options.B: Client hydration runs useEffect which fires the matchMedia listener and updates state
options.C: useMediaQuery uses synchronous XHR to check viewport
options.D: A cookie stores the viewport width between requests

cards.explanation: useMediaQuery initializes with default (false), then useEffect runs on client to call window.matchMedia and set the correct value.

cards.question: Which React 19 API pairs naturally with useDebounce for search input to keep UI responsive?

options.B: useTransition

cards.explanation: useTransition wraps the async search call as low-priority, keeping the input responsive while search results load.

cards.question: What advantage does React Compiler provide for custom hooks?

options.B: Auto-memoizes returned callbacks and values, removing useCallback/useMemo wrappers
options.C: Converts custom hooks to Server Actions
options.D: Adds runtime type checking to hook parameters

cards.explanation: React Compiler auto-memoizes all closures and values returned from hooks, making explicit useCallback/useMemo wrappers unnecessary.

cards.question: In a custom useLocalStorage wrapper with versioned schema migration, why is raw string comparison needed?

options.A: To detect cross-tab storage changes and re-parse with current migrations
options.B: To compress the stored JSON for performance
options.D: To check if the user has disabled cookies

cards.explanation: Comparing raw string detects changes made in other tabs via the storage event, triggering re-parsing with the latest migration chain.

cards.question: Which hook would you use to detect when a DOM element becomes visible for lazy loading?

options.B: useEventListener

cards.explanation: useIntersectionObserver wraps IntersectionObserver API to report when an element enters the viewport (or a specified root margin).

cards.question: Why would you wrap useMediaQuery in a custom hook with initializeWithValue option?

options.A: To improve SSR performance by skipping initial render
options.B: To skip the initial window.matchMedia call on first render to avoid hydration mismatch
options.C: To add analytics tracking for viewport changes

cards.explanation: Setting initializeWithValue: false returns false on first render, then useEffect sets real value. This avoids hydration mismatch when server-rendered HTML has a different initial value than client.

cards.question: Which hook from the Vercel AI SDK is used for multi-turn chat with message history?

options.B: useChat

cards.explanation: useChat manages message history, streaming, abort, and regenerate for conversational interfaces.

cards.question: How do you switch from OpenAI to Anthropic Claude with the Vercel AI SDK?

options.A: Change the API endpoint URL only
options.B: Install @ai-sdk/anthropic and change the model parameter passed to streamText
options.C: Rewrite all useChat calls to useCompletion
options.D: Anthropic is not supported by the AI SDK

cards.explanation: The AI SDK uses provider packages (@ai-sdk/openai, @ai-sdk/anthropic). Just install the provider and pass its model to streamText.

cards.question: What React 19 hook replaces the traditional form submission pattern for chat in the example?

options.B: useActionState

cards.explanation: useActionState manages form submission state and pending status, replacing manual handleSubmit + useState for loading.

cards.question: In the generative UI pattern, what does the AI model actually execute on the server?

options.B: A named tool/function with typed parameters

cards.explanation: The model calls a registered tool (function). The tool returns data. The client maps the data to a React component.

cards.question: What is the purpose of createStreamableValue in the Server Action chat pattern?

options.A: It creates a new OpenAI client instance
options.B: It wraps a stream so the client can read chunks progressively via async iteration
options.C: It compresses tokens for faster transfer

cards.explanation: createStreamableValue creates a stream that the client reads with for-await-of, enabling progressive UI updates.

cards.question: Which library is recommended for rendering streaming Markdown responses in the chat UI?

options.B: react-markdown

cards.explanation: react-markdown integrates as a React component that re-renders when content updates, ideal for streaming text.

cards.question: What does the stop() function returned by useChat do internally?

options.B: Aborts the underlying fetch request via AbortController

cards.explanation: useChat uses AbortController internally. stop() aborts the current fetch, halting the stream on the server.

cards.question: When should you use useCompletion instead of useChat?

options.A: When you need multi-turn conversation history
options.B: When you have a single prompt-response without chat history
options.D: When using Anthropic instead of OpenAI

cards.explanation: useCompletion is for single-turn completion tasks. useChat manages a conversation array with multiple exchanges.

cards.question: What React 19 feature enables a component to suspend while waiting for an async model response?

options.B: Suspense boundary with use() for promise consumption

cards.explanation: React 19's use() hook reads a promise within a Suspense boundary, suspending the component until the promise resolves.

cards.question: How does the AI SDK handle tool call serialization between server and client?

options.A: Tool calls are sent as raw JSX strings
options.B: The tool name + parameters are serialized in the stream; client maps to components
options.C: The server renders HTML and sends it to the client
options.D: Tool calls bypass the stream and use a separate WebSocket

cards.explanation: The stream contains structured tool invocation data (name, args, result). The client reads these and renders corresponding React components.

cards.question: What is the default rendering model for all components in the Next.js App Router?

options.B: Server Component

cards.explanation: All components in App Router are Server Components by default. 'use client' is required to opt into client-side interactivity.

cards.question: Which file convention provides Suspense fallback UI for a route segment?

options.B: loading.tsx

cards.explanation: loading.tsx automatically wraps the page in a Suspense boundary with the loading component as fallback.

cards.question: What is the difference between a Route Handler (app/api/*) and a Server Action?

options.B: Route Handlers work with external clients (mobile, curl); Server Actions are for form mutations within the same app
options.C: Server Actions are faster because they skip HTTP
options.D: Route Handlers cannot access the database

cards.explanation: Route Handlers serve external API consumers. Server Actions run on form submission within the app with revalidatePath for cache invalidation.

cards.question: What does revalidatePath do in a Server Action?

options.A: Clears the server-side cache for the given path, triggering re-fetch
options.B: Refreshes the client-side router cache only
options.C: Deletes the API route for that path
options.D: Redirects the user to a different page

cards.explanation: revalidatePath purges the server cache for the specified route, causing the next request to re-fetch data and regenerate the page.

cards.question: Which Next.js feature allows static pages to be regenerated on-demand after build time?

options.B: Incremental Static Regeneration (ISR)

cards.explanation: ISR with revalidate tag pre-builds pages at build time and re-generates them when stale requests come in.

cards.question: What React 19 hook replaces manual form submission state management in the Next.js Server Action pattern?

options.B: useActionState

cards.explanation: useActionState (formerly useFormState) manages form pending state and return values, integrating with Server Actions.

cards.question: How does Next.js detect that a page requires dynamic rendering?

options.A: The page must export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'
options.B: Using cookies(), headers(), or searchParams in the Server Component
options.C: Dynamic rendering is the default; opt out with static export
options.D: It checks if the component uses the useState hook

cards.explanation: Next.js automatically opts into dynamic rendering when Server Components use dynamic APIs like cookies(), headers(), or searchParams.

cards.question: What is the purpose of the matcher config in middleware.ts?

options.A: It defines which database tables the middleware can access
options.B: It specifies which routes trigger the middleware function
options.C: It sets the matching algorithm for path parameters
options.D: It configures the rate limiter for API routes

cards.explanation: The matcher config limits which paths execute the middleware, preventing unnecessary runs on static assets and API routes.

cards.question: How do you pass a ref to a Client Component from a Server Component in React 19?

options.A: Use React.forwardRef in the Client Component; Server Components pass ref directly
options.B: Ref is automatically forwarded for all components
options.C: Server Components cannot pass refs; only props are allowed across the boundary
options.D: Use the useImperativeHandle hook in the Server Component

cards.explanation: In React 19, ref is a regular prop. Client Components use forwardRef to receive it; Server Components pass it as any other prop.

cards.question: What does enabling experimental.reactCompiler in next.config.ts achieve?

options.A: Compiles JSX to plain JavaScript for older browsers
options.B: Auto-memoizes Server Actions and Client Component callbacks, reducing re-renders
options.C: Enables TypeScript strict mode for all components
options.D: Automatically converts Server Components to Client Components

cards.explanation: React Compiler auto-memoizes functions and values, eliminating manual useCallback/useMemo wrappers and reducing unnecessary re-renders.

cards.question: What is the primary purpose of Style Dictionary?

options.A: A CSS framework for responsive design
options.B: A build tool that transforms design tokens into platform-specific output
options.C: A design tool for creating color palettes
options.D: A runtime theme engine for React

cards.explanation: Style Dictionary takes structured token JSON and generates platform-specific files (CSS, JS, TS, Android XML, etc.) via transforms and formats.

cards.question: In the token hierarchy, what is the difference between alias tokens and component tokens?

options.A: Alias tokens are for JavaScript; component tokens are for CSS
options.B: Alias tokens map global tokens to semantic purposes; component tokens scope overrides to specific components
options.C: They are the same thing with different naming conventions
options.D: Alias tokens are deprecated; component tokens are the standard

cards.explanation: Alias tokens add semantic meaning (color.danger). Component tokens further scope values to specific components (button.background.danger).

cards.question: How do CSS custom properties enable runtime theming without re-render?

options.A: CSS custom properties trigger React re-render when changed
options.B: Custom properties resolve at paint time; changing data-theme attribute swaps values without component re-render
options.C: They use JavaScript setters to update the DOM
options.D: They are polyfilled by Style Dictionary at build time

cards.explanation: CSS custom properties are evaluated by the browser at paint time. Changing data-theme on a parent element cascades new values to all children without JavaScript re-render.

cards.question: What does a custom Style Dictionary transform do?

options.A: Changes the file format of the output
options.B: Modifies token values during build (e.g., px to dp, px to rem)

cards.explanation: Transforms are middleware that modify token values (or attributes) during build, enabling platform-specific value conversion.

cards.question: Why are static class names compiler-friendly for React Compiler?

options.A: Static class names are faster to hash
options.B: Static class names produce no dynamic style objects, so the compiler can memoize the rendering unconditionally
options.C: React Compiler only works with className strings, not objects
options.D: Static class names automatically tree-shake unused styles

cards.explanation: Static class names avoid dynamic style object creation. React Compiler can memoize the virtual DOM output without tracking style dependencies.

cards.question: What is the token reference syntax Style Dictionary uses for alias resolution?

options.B: {path.to.token}

cards.explanation: Style Dictionary uses {path.to.token} to reference other tokens. References are resolved at build time, not runtime.

cards.question: How does the CI token diff checker detect breaking changes?

options.A: Comparing file sizes of the output CSS
options.B: Parsing CSS custom properties from current and base branch, then checking for value changes on existing keys
options.C: Running the full Style Dictionary build twice
options.D: Comparing git commit hashes of token files

cards.explanation: The checker parses --variable: value pairs from CSS output on both branches. If an existing key has a different value, it's a breaking change.

cards.question: What is the benefit of Server Components consuming token CSS?

options.A: Server Components can modify CSS custom properties
options.B: Zero JS runtime cost for theming — CSS custom properties resolve at paint time on the client
options.C: Server Components inline all CSS into the HTML
options.D: Tokens are compiled to inline styles by the server

cards.explanation: Server Components emit HTML with class names. CSS custom properties handle theming at paint time. No JavaScript executes for token resolution.

cards.question: Which Style Dictionary format output would you use to generate TypeScript type definitions for tokens?

options.B: javascript/es6

cards.explanation: typescript/es6-declarations generates .d.ts type definitions alongside .js token files for TypeScript consumers.

cards.question: What scenario would require a custom Style Dictionary format instead of a built-in format?

options.A: Generating CSS custom properties for the web
options.B: Generating SCSS variables for a Sass project
options.C: Generating a Tailwind CSS config extension from tokens

cards.explanation: Tailwind config is a custom JSON structure not covered by built-in formats. A custom format function transforms tokens into the Tailwind extend config shape.

cards.question: How do you add Framer Motion animations to a third-party Button component?

options.A: Wrap with motion.div — but lose Button's built-in functionality
options.B: Use motion(Button) — returns a motion-enhanced component
options.C: Button does not support animation — use CSS instead
options.D: Copy Button internals into a motion component

cards.answer_text: Use motion(Button) — returns a motion-enhanced component
cards.explanation: motion(Component) wraps any component with motion capabilities — forwards ref, accepts motion props, preserves original component's API.

cards.question: What is the purpose of AnimatePresence?

options.B: Plays exit animations when a child component unmounts, then removes it from DOM
options.C: Provides animation presets for common patterns
options.D: Detects when animated elements are visible in viewport

cards.answer_text: Plays exit animations when a child component unmounts, then removes it from DOM
cards.explanation: AnimatePresence tracks children by key. When key disappears from render output, Framer Motion plays the exit animation defined on that element, then unmounts it.

cards.question: With AnimatePresence mode='popLayout', what happens during exit?

options.A: Exiting element maintains its layout space until animation completes
options.B: Exiting element immediately removes its layout — remaining elements animate to fill gap
options.C: Exit and enter animations play simultaneously
options.D: popLayout is not a valid mode

cards.answer_text: Exiting element immediately removes its layout — remaining elements animate to fill gap
cards.explanation: popLayout removes layout space immediately on exit, so surrounding elements animate to new positions without waiting. sync plays enter/exit simultaneously. wait plays exit then enter.

cards.question: What does layoutId enable in Framer Motion?

options.B: Shares identity between separate components so Framer Motion animates position/size change as same element
options.C: Assigns layout coordinates to motion elements

cards.answer_text: Shares identity between separate components so Framer Motion animates position/size change as same element
cards.explanation: layoutId tells Framer Motion that two separate components (e.g., thumbnail and modal image) represent same element. Position/size transitions animate smoothly instead of mount/unmount.

cards.question: How to respect user's reduced-motion preference in Framer Motion?

options.B: Use useReducedMotion() hook — conditionally render non-animated fallback

cards.answer_text: Use useReducedMotion() hook — conditionally render non-animated fallback
cards.explanation: useReducedMotion() reads prefers-reduced-motion media query. Wrapper conditionally renders children without motion props when user prefers reduced motion.

cards.question: Which animation property avoids triggering layout recalculations?

options.B: height

cards.answer_text: transform
cards.explanation: Transform and opacity are GPU-composited — they do not trigger layout or paint recalculations. Width, height, top, left all trigger layout recalc on each frame.

cards.question: Two list items swap positions but share same key. What happens?

options.A: AnimatePresence plays exit animation for first, enter for second
options.B: Framer Motion treats them as same element — layout animates position change without mount/unmount
options.C: Items do not animate — keys must be unique
options.D: Both items animate independently to new positions

cards.answer_text: Framer Motion treats them as same element — layout animates position change without mount/unmount
cards.explanation: Same key = same element identity. Framer Motion uses layout animation (if layout prop set) to move element from old to new position. AnimatePresence is not involved in same-key swaps.

cards.question: What does variants' staggerChildren property do?

options.A: Animates each child with a delay relative to previous child's animation start
options.B: Groups children into animation batches
options.C: Randomizes animation order for visual variety

cards.answer_text: Animates each child with a delay relative to previous child's animation start
cards.explanation: staggerChildren creates cascading delay: child 1 animates, then child 2 after staggerChildren delay, then child 3, etc. delayChildren adds initial delay before first child starts.

cards.question: Which transition type produces natural-feeling UI animations?

options.B: spring — natural motion based on physics (stiffness, damping, mass)

cards.answer_text: spring — natural motion based on physics (stiffness, damping, mass)
cards.explanation: Spring produces natural feel because real-world objects have mass, stiffness, and damping. Tween is predictable but can feel robotic. Inertia is for drag deceleration. Keyframes for multi-step.

cards.question: When wrapping a third-party component with motion(), what must be forwarded?

options.B: ref — motion components require ref forwarding for layout measurement and gesture tracking

cards.answer_text: ref — motion components require ref forwarding for layout measurement and gesture tracking
cards.explanation: motion() requires forwarded ref for internal layout measurement, gesture detection, and scroll tracking. Without ref forwarding, motion props like layout, drag, and whileInView will not work.

cards.question: How does TanStack Router define route relationships?

options.A: Flat config object with nested path strings
options.B: Route tree — each Route instance declares its parent via getParentRoute()
options.D: JSON route manifest imported at build time

cards.answer_text: Route tree — each Route instance declares its parent via getParentRoute()
cards.explanation: Each Route declares getParentRoute() returning its parent route. Tree structure enables full type inference: params, search, loader data flow down the tree with correct types.

cards.question: What happens when a search param fails zod validation?

options.B: Invalid param is silently dropped
options.C: Depends on schema method — catch() returns fallback, otherwise route error boundary catches
options.D: Search params are not validated at runtime

cards.answer_text: Depends on schema method — catch() returns fallback, otherwise route error boundary catches
cards.explanation: catch() returns fallback for invalid values. Without catch, validation error triggers route error boundary — errorComponent renders. This prevents malformed URLs from breaking the app.

cards.question: How does a parent layout render its child route content?

options.A: Props.children — child component is passed as children
options.B: <Outlet /> — renders active child route component
options.D: Child route is rendered automatically without explicit placeholder

cards.answer_text: <Outlet /> — renders active child route component
cards.explanation: Parent route component renders <Outlet /> as placeholder. TanStack Router injects matched child route's component into Outlet. Supports arbitrary nesting depth.

cards.question: What does beforeLoad return to redirect user to login?

options.B: Return { redirect: '/login' }

cards.answer_text: Throw router.navigate({ to: '/login' })
cards.explanation: Throwing router.navigate() from beforeLoad aborts current navigation and redirects. TanStack Router catches the navigate throw in beforeLoad and executes the redirect.

cards.question: How to access loader data in a route component?

options.B: useLoaderData() — untyped, must cast

cards.answer_text: Route.useLoaderData() — returns typed loader data
cards.explanation: Route.useLoaderData() is a hook on the route instance. Return type matches loader's return type — full inference. No casting needed.

cards.question: How does TanStack Router handle same-route param changes (e.g., product/1 to product/2)?

options.B: Keeps component mounted — useParams hook updates reactively. beforeLoad does not re-run.
options.C: Runs beforeLoad again for each param change

cards.answer_text: Keeps component mounted — useParams hook updates reactively. beforeLoad does not re-run.
cards.explanation: Param changes within same route pattern keep component mounted. useParams and useSearch reactively update. beforeLoad only runs on route activation, not param changes within same route.

cards.question: What is the purpose of loaderDeps in a route definition?

options.A: Declares dependencies from search/params that should trigger loader re-run
options.B: Lists npm dependencies the route needs
options.D: Specifies static paths the loader depends on

cards.answer_text: Declares dependencies from search/params that should trigger loader re-run
cards.explanation: loaderDeps returns a memoized object from search/params. When deps change (same route, different search), loader re-runs. Without loaderDeps, loader only runs on route activation.

cards.question: How to code-split a route component in TanStack Router?

options.B: Use React.lazy(() => import('./page')) as route component
options.C: Split route definitions into separate files

cards.answer_text: Use React.lazy(() => import('./page')) as route component
cards.explanation: Route component accepts React.lazy() result. Combine with pendingComponent for loading UI and wrapInSuspense: true for Suspense integration.

cards.question: Which hook provides access to current route's search params?

options.B: Route.useSearch() — returns typed search object from validateSearch schema

cards.answer_text: Route.useSearch() — returns typed search object from validateSearch schema
cards.explanation: Route.useSearch() returns validated search object matching the route's validateSearch schema. TypeScript infers exact shape — no type assertions.

cards.question: How does TanStack Router handle route context injection?

options.A: React Context provider nested in route tree
options.B: Router constructor context option — typed via module augmentation (Register interface)

cards.answer_text: Router constructor context option — typed via module augmentation (Register interface)
cards.explanation: Context is passed to Router constructor and typed via declare module '@tanstack/react-router' { interface Register { router: typeof router } }. beforeLoad and loaders access context through their parameters.

cards.question: How should Uppy instance lifecycle be managed in React?

options.A: Create new Uppy() on every render — close in useEffect
options.B: useMemo with empty deps for creation, useEffect return for cleanup
options.C: Global singleton — create once at module level
options.D: React ref with useEffect for creation and cleanup

cards.answer_text: useMemo with empty deps for creation, useEffect return for cleanup
cards.explanation: useMemo preserves Uppy instance across renders. Empty deps = create once. useEffect return calls uppy.close() to clean up files, events, and DOM on unmount.

cards.question: What problem does Tus protocol solve?

options.B: Resumable uploads — file upload continues from last confirmed chunk after interruption

cards.answer_text: Resumable uploads — file upload continues from last confirmed chunk after interruption
cards.explanation: Tus splits uploads into chunks. Client tracks which chunks server acknowledged. On resume, client sends only unacknowledged chunks. Protocol is standardized (tus.io).

cards.question: What is Uppy Companion?

options.A: Browser extension for debugging Uppy uploads
options.B: Server-side middleware that proxies remote file providers (Google Drive, Dropbox)
options.C: Alternative to Uppy Dashboard with different UI
options.D: File compression service for images before upload

cards.answer_text: Server-side middleware that proxies remote file providers (Google Drive, Dropbox)
cards.explanation: Companion is Node.js server middleware that handles OAuth flows and file streaming for remote providers. Files stream directly from provider to upload destination without passing through companion.

cards.question: How to restrict file types before upload starts?

options.A: Validate in server endpoint — reject response
options.B: Uppy restrictions.allowedFileTypes filters file picker and file drop
options.C: CSS hidden input accept attribute only
options.D: TypeScript type checking on File object

cards.answer_text: Uppy restrictions.allowedFileTypes filters file picker and file drop
cards.explanation: allowedFileTypes filters both file dialog (accept attribute) and programmatic/file-drop additions. Uses glob patterns: 'image/*', '.pdf'. onBeforeFileAdded for additional custom validation.

cards.question: What is the disadvantage of relying on Uppy Dashboard's built-in progress UI?

options.B: Couples upload UI to Uppy — switching upload library requires replacing UI
options.C: Dashboard does not support percentage display
options.D: No disadvantage — Dashboard UI is fully customizable

cards.answer_text: Couples upload UI to Uppy — switching upload library requires replacing UI
cards.explanation: Dashboard provides complete UI including progress, preview, retry. Switching to different upload library means replacing all Dashboard-related code. App-level upload state tracker decouples UI from library.

cards.question: Which Uppy event provides real-time per-file upload percentage?

options.B: upload-progress — provides bytesUploaded and bytesTotal per file

cards.answer_text: upload-progress — provides bytesUploaded and bytesTotal per file
cards.explanation: upload-progress fires per file with { bytesUploaded, bytesTotal }. Calculate percentage: Math.round(bytesUploaded / bytesTotal * 100). total-progress provides aggregate across all files.

cards.question: When should you self-host Uppy Companion instead of using Uppy Cloud?

options.B: When OAuth credentials, data sovereignty, or production-scale reliability is required
options.D: Uppy Cloud is the only option — Companion is deprecated

cards.answer_text: When OAuth credentials, data sovereignty, or production-scale reliability is required
cards.explanation: Self-hosting gives control over OAuth secrets, data flow, and scaling. Uppy Cloud is convenient for prototyping but imposes rate limits and shares OAuth credentials across tenants.

cards.question: What does onBeforeFileAdded return to reject a file?

options.B: Return false
options.D: Call reject() on the file object

cards.answer_text: Return false
cards.explanation: onBeforeFileAdded returns false to reject file. Use uppy.info() to show user-friendly error message. Can also modify file object (e.g., add metadata) before returning true.

cards.question: Why might Tus not be ideal for serverless environments?

options.A: Tus does not work with HTTP
options.B: Tus requires persistent storage for upload state — serverless functions are stateless
options.C: Tus only works with Node.js servers
options.D: Tus is deprecated in favor of multipart uploads

cards.answer_text: Tus requires persistent storage for upload state — serverless functions are stateless
cards.explanation: Tus upload protocol expects server to store chunk state on disk or database. Serverless functions (Vercel, Lambda) are ephemeral. Workaround: use @uppy/aws-s3 or @uppy/multipart for serverless.

cards.question: How does Uppy handle retry after upload failure?

options.B: Tus plugin retries with configured delays. Server must support Tus protocol for resume.
options.D: Retry is handled by browser's built-in retry mechanism

cards.answer_text: Tus plugin retries with configured delays. Server must support Tus protocol for resume.
cards.explanation: Tus plugin configures retryDelays array. On failure, Uppy waits then retries. If server supports Tus resume, it continues from last chunk. If server does not, it restarts from beginning.

cards.question: How is Sonner's Toaster component configured in an app?

options.A: Wraps each component that needs notifications
options.B: Single instance placed once in app root — all toasts render from it
options.C: Toaster is automatically injected by Sonner — no component needed
options.D: Each toast call creates its own Toaster instance

cards.answer_text: Single instance placed once in app root — all toasts render from it
cards.explanation: Place <Toaster /> once at app root (App.tsx, layout.tsx). All toast.success/error/info calls render into this Toaster. Position, styling, expand mode configured here.

cards.question: What does toast.promise() return?

options.A: A promise that resolves with toast ID
options.B: The same promise passed in — toast.promise does not block
options.D: A new promise that resolves when toast is dismissed

cards.answer_text: A promise that resolves with toast ID
cards.explanation: toast.promise returns a promise that resolves with the toast ID string/number. The original promise runs in parallel — the function does not await it.

cards.question: What is the benefit of typed notification helpers (notifySuccess, notifyError)?

options.A: No benefit — direct toast calls are simpler
options.B: Enforces consistent duration, dismissible behavior, and action button style across app

cards.answer_text: Enforces consistent duration, dismissible behavior, and action button style across app
cards.explanation: Typed helpers centralize toast configuration: error toasts get longer duration, all toasts get consistent action button styling, dismissible behavior is standardized. Changing app-wide behavior changes one file.

cards.question: How does Sonner handle multiple toasts stacking?

options.A: Only one toast visible at a time
options.B: Toasts stack vertically, newest at top. visibleToasts prop limits count.
options.D: Toasts replace each other — only latest stays

cards.answer_text: Toasts stack vertically, newest at top. visibleToasts prop limits count.
cards.explanation: Default stacking: newest on top, older below. visibleToasts (default 3) limits simultaneous display. Excess queue until dismissal. Expand mode shows full content stacks.

cards.question: When would you use expand={true} on Toaster?

options.A: Always — expanded mode is better UX
options.B: When toasts contain important information that should not be truncated

cards.answer_text: When toasts contain important information that should not be truncated
cards.explanation: Expanded mode shows toast content in full vertical layout. Good for: error details, multiple action buttons, rich content. Stacked mode better for high-frequency updates.

cards.question: How to update a toast after it's shown?

options.A: Cannot update — dismiss and show new
options.B: Call toast.success(newMsg, { id: existingToastId })

cards.answer_text: Call toast.success(newMsg, { id: existingToastId })
cards.explanation: Pass existing toast ID to toast.success/error/info to update content. Pattern: const id = toast.loading('Uploading...'); later toast.success('Done', { id }). Updating dismisses loading and shows success.

cards.question: How does Sonner integrate with React 19 useActionState?

options.B: Call toast in server action result handler — useEffect reacts to state change
options.C: Server actions cannot call Sonner — client-only
options.D: Wrap useActionState with Sonner's withToast() higher-order function

cards.answer_text: Call toast in server action result handler — useEffect reacts to state change
cards.explanation: Call toast from client component. Server action returns result object. Client useEffect watches state changes and calls appropriate toast helper. toast itself is client-side only.

cards.question: How to create a toast with custom JSX content in Sonner?

options.B:
Use toast.custom((t) =>
...
) — receives toast ID for dismiss
options.C: Pass JSX as first argument to toast.success

cards.answer_text:
Use toast.custom((t) =>
...
) — receives toast ID for dismiss
cards.explanation: toast.custom() accepts render function. Receives toast ID as argument for custom dismiss behavior. Used for complex layouts with buttons, icons, and formatted text.

cards.question: How to style Sonner toasts using CSS?

options.A: Sonner does not support custom styling
options.B: CSS custom properties (--sonner-normal-bg, --sonner-success-bg, etc.) on Toaster container
options.C: Inline styles on toast.success() call only
options.D: CSS modules imported from Sonner package

cards.answer_text: CSS custom properties (--sonner-normal-bg, --sonner-success-bg, etc.) on Toaster container
cards.explanation: Sonner exposes CSS custom properties on the Toaster element. Override: --sonner-normal-bg, --sonner-normal-text, --sonner-success-bg, --sonner-error-bg, --sonner-radius, etc.

cards.question: How should toast duration differ between success and error?

options.B: Error toasts should be longer (5-8s) than success (2-4s) — user needs more time to read error
options.C: Success should be longer — user wants to celebrate
options.D: Duration does not matter — toasts auto-dismiss on click

cards.answer_text: Error toasts should be longer (5-8s) than success (2-4s) — user needs more time to read error
cards.explanation: Errors need longer duration because user must read and understand failure. Action button (Retry, Undo) increases duration further. Success toasts disappear faster — positive feedback is lightweight.

cards.question: What is the purpose of cmdk's CommandInput component?

options.A: Renders a styled text input that filters CommandItems by default
options.B: Provides a search bar but filtering must be implemented manually

cards.answer_text: Renders a styled text input that filters CommandItems by default
cards.explanation: CommandInput renders a search input that automatically filters CommandItem children by their value and keywords props. onValueChange callback provides custom filtering alternative.

cards.question: How does cmdk rank search results by default?

options.B: Exact match > prefix match > substring match > keyword match
options.D: By order items appear in DOM

cards.answer_text: Exact match > prefix match > substring match > keyword match
cards.explanation: cmdk's built-in filter ranks: exact value match highest, then prefix match, then substring, then keyword match. Helps users find common actions quickly.

cards.question: What pattern enables feature modules to add their own commands without modifying a central file?

options.A: Central commands.tsx file with all commands
options.B: Command registration pattern — modules call registry.register() at init

cards.answer_text: Command registration pattern — modules call registry.register() at init
cards.explanation: CommandRegistry singleton accepts register() calls from any module. Each module calls register() during initialization. Registry aggregates and provides commands to CommandMenu component.

cards.question: How to implement a submenu/nested command in cmdk?

options.A: cmdk does not support nested commands
options.B: Render separate Command tree when user selects parent item — swap visible content

cards.answer_text: Render separate Command tree when user selects parent item — swap visible content
cards.explanation: Submenus are separate Command trees rendered conditionally. Parent item's onSelect sets state to show submenu. Submenu includes back button to return to main menu.

cards.question: What is the difference between Dialog and Inline mode in cmdk?

options.A: Dialog mode renders overlay with backdrop. Inline embeds command list in page flow.
options.B: No difference — same functionality
options.C: Dialog mode has search, Inline does not

cards.answer_text: Dialog mode renders overlay with backdrop. Inline embeds command list in page flow.
cards.explanation: CommandDialog wraps Command in a modal dialog (overlay, backdrop, focus trap). Inline mode renders Command directly in DOM for embedded use cases.

cards.question: When should you set shouldFilter={false} on Command?

options.B: When implementing custom filtering logic external to cmdk
options.C: When CommandItems have no value prop
